name: Short Stoires Collect(Grade)
desc: A collection of short stories of various authors ( simplied version )
author: Various

paragraphs.content: Near a park called Washington Square, there are streets that don't go straight. They split into tiny roads named 'places.' These little roads twist and turn. One road even loops around so it crosses over itself. An artist found something interesting about this road. Imagine if someone who needed to pay for art supplies walked this funny path and bumped into himself, still not having paid for his things!

Some artists went to a place called Greenwich Village because they liked the old buildings and the cheap places to live. They brought with them some metal cups and cooking dishes from another street and started living there together like a small village.

Sue and Johnsy lived in a short, red brick building with three floors. They had a special room there where they made art. 'Johnsy' was a nickname for Joanna. One friend came from a place called Maine and the other from a place called California. They became friends while eating at a big table in a restaurant on Eighth Street. They liked the same kind of art, a type of salad, and a style of big, puffy sleeves on dresses. Because they liked these things so much, they decided to share the art room together.

It happened in May. Then in November, a cold sickness that doctors call Pneumonia came like a hidden stranger. It went around touching some people with its cold hands. In the east part of the town, this sickness walked around easily and made lots of people sick. But in the small, twisty streets, it moved slowly.

Mr. Pneumonia was not a kind old man. He was tough and not fair when he chose to bother a small, weak lady who was not strong because of the warm California winds. But he made Johnsy very sick, and she stayed in bed, barely able to move. She looked out of her little window at the plain brick wall of the house next door.
One morning, a doctor who had a lot to do asked Sue to come into the hall. He had a big, fluffy, gray eyebrow.

He said she has one chance to get better out of ten. He was using a special tool to check her temperature. He thinks the most important thing is that she really wants to get better. Sometimes people act like they're ready to give up, and that makes all the medicine seem not useful. The sick girl doesn't think she will get well. Is she worried about something?
Sue said that she wanted to paint a picture of the sea at Naples one day.
Paint? No way! Does she really have something important to think about again and again—like a boy, maybe?
Sue asked with a funny sound in her voice, 'A man? Is a man important? But no, doctor, it's not like that.'

The doctor said, 'It's because she's weak. I'll try my best to make her better with what I know from science. But if she starts thinking about her own big goodbye ceremony, I believe the medicine won't work as well. If you can make her interested in talking about new winter clothes, then I think she has a better chance to get well.'
After the doctor left, Sue went to another room and cried a lot into a paper towel. Then she walked into Johnsy's room, pretending to be happy, with her drawing stuff, and whistled a fun tune.
Johnsy was in bed, very still, looking out the window. Sue stopped making a whistling sound because she thought Johnsy was sleeping.

She got her paper ready and started to make a picture with a pen for a story in a magazine. Young artists make pictures for stories to help them become better at art. Young writers write these stories to get better at writing.
Sue was drawing fancy pants and a small glass for one eye on a cowboy from Idaho. She heard a quiet noise again and again. She ran to the bed to check.
Johnsy had her eyes open really big. She was looking through the window and counting numbers backwards.
She counted down: 'twelve,' then a bit after, 'eleven.' Next came 'ten' and 'nine.' Right after, she said 'eight' and 'seven' quickly, one after the other.

Sue looked out the window carefully. What could she see? There was just a plain yard with nothing in it, and the side of a brick house very close. There was an old ivy plant with twisted and old roots going up half of the brick wall. The chilly fall air made most of the leaves fall off the plant. Now, it looked like just branches holding on to the old bricks.
Sue asked, 'What's wrong, sweetie?'

Johnsy spoke very softly. 'Six,' she said. 'The leaves are dropping quickly. Three days ago, there were so many, almost a hundred! It hurt my head to count all of them. But counting is simple now. Oh, look! Another leaf fell. Just five are left.'
What five things, sweetie? Tell me, Sudie.
Leaves fall from the ivy plant. When the last one drops, I have to leave. I've known this for three days. Didn't the doctor say that?

Sue said, 'That's silly. Why do you think leaves from the plant outside will help you feel better? You used to like that plant. Don't be silly. The doctor said this morning that you have a really good chance to get well soon. It's like when we are safe in the city. Now try to drink some soup, so I can go draw a picture to sell. With the money, I can buy special juice to help you get better and food for me.'
Johnsy said, 'Don't bring more wine. Look, another leaf fell. No, I don't want soup. Only four leaves left. I want to watch the last one fall before night. Then I'll leave, too.'

Sue said to her friend, 'Please close your eyes and don't peek outside until I finish my drawing. I have to give it to someone tomorrow. I need to see well to draw, or else I would close the curtain.'
Can you draw in the other room?
Sue said, 'I want to stay with you. And I don't like you looking at those silly leaves.'

Johnsy said, 'Please tell me when you are done.' She closed her eyes and lay very still, like a statue that had tipped over. 'I want to see the last leaf fall. I don't want to wait anymore. I don't want to think anymore. I just want to let go of everything and float down like one of those leaves that is too tired to hang on to the tree.'
Sue said, 'Go to sleep. I need to get Behrman to come and pretend to be the old miner for my picture. I'll be right back. Don't move until I return.'

Mr. Behrman was an old man who liked to paint and lived in the apartment under the two young painters. He had a big, bushy beard and was over sixty years old. Mr. Behrman tried to be a great artist for forty years but never made a painting that people thought was really special. He only made small paintings for shops sometimes. He didn't have much money, so he would sit still so other painters could paint him and give him a little money. Mr. Behrman liked to drink a strong drink called gin, and he always talked about making an amazing painting one day. He was a tough little man who didn't like it when people were not strong. He felt it was his job to look after the two young painters who lived above him.

Sue went to Behrman's room which was not very bright and smelled like berries. She saw an empty painting board that had been ready for a long time, but no painting was started on it. She talked to Behrman about her friend Johnsy's strange idea. Sue was worried that Johnsy might become very sick and weak, just like a tiny leaf that can be blown away by the wind.
Old man Behrman, with his eyes looking very red and watery, yelled loudly because he thought such silly ideas were very silly.

He shouted, 'Wow!' Are there really people who are so silly that they would stop living just because leaves fall off a vine? I've never heard of that. No, I won't pretend to be a model for your silly hermit idea. Why do you let that nonsense into her head? Oh, that poor little Miss Johnsy.'

Sue said, 'She is really sick and doesn't feel strong. She's hot and is thinking of weird things. It's okay, Mr. Behrman, if you don't want to sit still for my picture. But I think you're not being very nice.'

Behrman shouted, 'You act like a lady! Who says I won't help? Let's go. I've been trying to tell you for half an hour that I'm ready to help. This is not a good place for Miss Yohnsy to be sick. One day, I will paint a very special picture, and then we can all leave this place. Yes, indeed.'

Johnsy was asleep when they walked up the stairs. Sue made the room dark by pulling down the shade, and she quietly asked Behrman to come into the next room. They both looked out the window at the plant with little leaves, feeling worried. It was raining hard and there was some snow too. Behrman, wearing his old blue shirt, sat down like he was a miner looking for gold, but he used a big pot instead of a rock to sit on.
When Sue woke up after sleeping for an hour in the morning, she saw Johnsy with tired eyes looking at the green curtain.
Please lift it; I need to look,
Sue did what she was told, even though she was very tired.

Look! After a lot of rain and strong wind all night, there was still one green leaf on the wall. It was the only one left on the plant. It was still green where it was attached, but the edges looked a little yellow like it was getting old. It was holding on tight to a branch high up, about as tall as two giraffes.
Johnsy said, 'This is the last leaf. I thought it would fall when it was dark because the wind was blowing. It will fall today, and I think I will go away like the leaf.'
Sue said softly, 'Please think about me, if you don't want to think about yourself. What would happen to me?'

But Johnsy did not speak. The saddest thing ever is when someone's heart is getting ready for a secret trip far away. Johnsy felt this even more as she slowly stopped feeling close to her friends and the world.

The day went by, and when it started to get dark, they could still see the one green leaf stuck to its stem on the wall. When night came, the cold north wind blew hard again, and the rain kept hitting the windows and falling down from the edges of the roof.
When it was bright, Johnsy told someone to lift up the window shade.
The green leaf from the ivy plant did not go away.
Johnsy was in bed for a long time just watching. Then she shouted to Sue, who was making soup on the cooker.

Johnsy told Sudie, 'I did something wrong. The last leaf didn't fall because it wants to tell me I shouldn't have wanted to stop living. It's not good to feel that way. Can you please get me some soup and milk with a bit of sweet wine? But first, I'd like to look at myself in a small mirror. Then, put some pillows around me so I can sit up and see you make the food.'
One hour after, she spoke.
Sudie, one day I want to make a picture of the sea near Naples.
In the afternoon, the doctor visited. When he went out, Sue found a reason to go to the hallway.

The doctor held Sue's hand gently and said, 'It's like flipping a coin. With someone to take care of you, you can get better. Now, I need to go see another sick person below. His name is Behrman. He likes to make art. He's also very sick with a bad lung problem. He's old and not strong, and he can't get better. But he's going to the hospital so he can feel a little better.'
The next day, the doctor told Sue: 'She is safe now. You did it. She just needs good food and care.'
That afternoon, Sue went to Johnsy's bed. Johnsy was happy and making a scarf out of blue wool that wasn't really needed. Sue hugged her, even with the pillows around her.

I need to tell you something, little white mouse. Mr. Behrman got very sick with a cold in his lungs and he died today at the hospital. He was only sick for two days. The person who takes care of the building found him in his room downstairs. He was in a lot of pain and his clothes were very wet and cold. No one knew why he was out on such a bad, cold night. Then they saw a light, a ladder that was moved, some paintbrushes, and colors mixed on a board. Now, look out the window at the green leaf on the wall. Did you ever think why it didn't move in the wind? That's because Mr. Behrman painted it there when the real last leaf fell.

One dollar and eighty-seven cents. That was all Della had. Sixty cents of it was in pennies saved from her grocery and meat shopping. She felt embarrassed for saving so little money. Three times Della counted it. One dollar and eighty-seven cents. And tomorrow was Christmas.
There was nothing else to do but lie down on the worn-out couch and cry, so Della did. Life, she thought, is full of crying, little sniffling, and smiles, with more crying than anything.

While Della was crying less, let’s look at their home. It was a small apartment costing $8 per week. It wasn't terrible but far from nice. In the hallway below, there was a mailbox that never had letters and a doorbell that didn’t work. There was a card with the name “Mr. James Dillingham Young.”

The “Dillingham” was added when James made more money, $30 per week. Now, with only $20 a week, they thought of shortening it to just “Mr. D.” When James came home, he was just “Jim” to Della, and she hugged him a lot.

Della finished crying and wiped her face with a powder cloth. She stood by the window and looked out at a gray cat walking on a gray fence in a gray backyard. Tomorrow would be Christmas, and she only had $1.87 to buy Jim a present. She had saved every penny for months, but expenses were always more than she expected. Only $1.87 to buy something for Jim. Her Jim. She had spent many happy moments thinking of what nice gift to get him.

There was a long, thin mirror between the windows of the room. It was broken, so you could only see strips of yourself. Della, being slim, knew how to use it to see her reflection. Suddenly, she turned away from the window and stood in front of the mirror. Her eyes were shining, but her face turned pale. Quickly, she let down her hair. It was long, beautiful, and reached below her knees, almost like a dress.

Jim had a gold watch from his father and grandfather. And Della had her hair. Both of them were proud of these things. If Queen Sheba lived nearby, Della would hang her hair out the window to show off. If King Solomon was the landlord, Jim would show him his watch.

Della’s beautiful hair fell down like brown water. It was so long it almost covered her. Then she put it up again nervously. She stood still for a moment and cried again. She put on her old brown jacket and hat. With a quick turn, she left the apartment and walked to the street.

She stopped at a shop with a sign that read “Mme. Sofronie. Hair Goods of All Kinds.” Della ran upstairs and caught her breath. Madame Sofronie, a large and pale woman, was there. “Will you buy my hair?” asked Della. “I buy hair,” said Madame. “Take off your hat and let’s see it.”
Della’s brown hair fell down. “Twenty dollars,” said Madame, lifting the hair with her hand. “Give it to me quickly,” said Della.

With $20 in her pocket, Della happily searched the stores for Jim’s present. She found it at last. It was perfect for Jim and no one else. It was a platinum watch chain, simple and elegant. It showed its value by being well-made, not flashy. It was worthy of Jim's watch.

Della bought the chain for $21 and hurried home with 87 cents. With this chain, Jim could proudly show his watch. At home, Della’s excitement turned to practicality. She got out her curling irons, lit the gas, and worked to fix her hair. This was a hard job.
After forty minutes, her head was covered with tiny curls, making her look like a schoolboy. She looked at herself in the mirror carefully.

“If Jim doesn’t get angry,” Della said to herself, “before he takes a second look at me, he’ll say I look like a chorus girl from Coney Island. But what could I do—oh, what could I do with just one dollar and eighty-seven cents?”

At 7 o’clock, the coffee was ready, and the frying pan was on the stove, hot and ready to cook the meat. Jim was never late. Della held the watch chain tightly in her hand and sat on the corner of the table near the door. She heard his steps on the stairs, and her face turned pale for a moment. She often said a little silent prayer about simple things, and now she whispered, “Please God, make him think I am still pretty.”
The door opened, and Jim walked in and closed it. He looked thin and serious. Poor Jim, he was only twenty-two and had so many responsibilities. He needed a new coat and didn’t have gloves.

Jim stopped inside the door, as still as a statue. He stared at Della with an expression she couldn’t understand, and it scared her. It wasn’t anger, surprise, disapproval, or horror. He just stared at her with that strange look on his face.
Della got off the table and went to him.

“Jim, darling,” she cried, “don’t look at me like that. I cut off my hair and sold it because I couldn’t celebrate Christmas without giving you a present. It’ll grow out again—you won’t mind, will you? I just had to do it. My hair grows really fast. Say ‘Merry Christmas,’ Jim, and let’s be happy. You don’t know what a nice gift I’ve got for you.”
“You cut off your hair?” asked Jim slowly, as if he couldn’t believe it.
“Cut it off and sold it,” said Della. “Don’t you like me just as much? I’m still me without my hair, right?”
Jim looked around the room in confusion.
“You say your hair is gone?” he said, almost like he couldn’t comprehend it.

“You don’t have to look for it,” said Della. “It’s sold—gone. It’s Christmas Eve, Jim. Be nice to me because I did it for you. Maybe my hair was numbered,” she said sweetly, “but no one could ever count my love for you. Shall I cook the meat, Jim?”

Jim seemed to wake up from his trance. He hugged Della. For the next ten seconds, let’s look away to give them some privacy. Whether they have eight dollars a week or a million a year doesn’t matter. A mathematician or a clever person might give you the wrong answer. The Magi brought valuable gifts, but love was not among them. This will be explained later.

It seemed like a good idea, but let me tell you what happened. Bill Driscoll and I were in Alabama when we thought of the kidnapping plan. Later, Bill said it was during a moment of bad thinking, but we didn’t know that at first.

There was a small town called Summit. It was very flat and had simple, happy people. Bill and I had six hundred dollars, but we needed two thousand more for a fake land deal in Illinois. We discussed it at our hotel. We thought that people in small towns love their children very much, so kidnapping would work better there. We knew Summit only had weak police and maybe some lazy dogs to chase us.

We picked our victim: a boy, the only child of a man named Ebenezer Dorset. Ebenezer was a strict, serious man who loved money. The boy was ten years old, with lots of freckles and reddish-brown hair. We thought Ebenezer would pay two thousand dollars to get his son back. But wait until you hear what happened.

About two miles from Summit, there was a small mountain with lots of cedar trees. On the back of this mountain, there was a cave where we stored our supplies. One evening, we drove past old Dorset’s house in a buggy. The boy was outside, throwing rocks at a kitten.
"Hey, little boy," Bill said, "would you like some candy and a nice ride?"
The boy threw a piece of brick and hit Bill in the eye.

"That will cost the old man an extra five hundred dollars," Bill said as he climbed over the wheel. The boy fought like a wild animal, but we finally got him into the buggy and drove away. We took him to the cave and I tied up the horse in the trees. After dark, I returned the buggy to the village three miles away and walked back.

Bill was covering his scratches and bruises with bandages. There was a fire by the big rock at the cave entrance, and the boy was watching a pot of boiling coffee with two bird feathers in his red hair. When I approached, he pointed a stick at me and said, “Ha! cursed paleface, do you dare to enter the camp of Red Chief, the terror of the plains?”
“He’s fine now,” said Bill, checking his legs for bruises. “We’re playing Indian. I’m Old Hank, the Trapper, Red Chief’s prisoner, and he’s going to scalp me at sunrise. That kid kicks really hard.”

Yes, that boy seemed to be having the time of his life. Camping in the cave made him forget he was kidnapped. He called me Snake-eye, the Spy, and said his warriors would burn me at sunrise when they returned from their hunt.

Then we had dinner. The boy, with his mouth full of bacon, bread, and gravy, started talking nonstop: “I like this. Never camped before. Had a pet 'possum once. I’m nine years old. Hate school. Rats ate Jimmy Talbot’s aunt’s hen’s eggs. Are there real Indians here? More gravy, please. Do moving trees make wind? We had five puppies. Why is your nose red, Hank? My dad has lots of money. Are stars hot? I beat Ed Walker twice. Don’t like girls. You can’t catch toads without a string. Do oxen make noise? Why are oranges round? Do we have beds? Amos Murray has six toes. Parrots talk, but monkeys or fish can’t. How many make twelve?”
He often remembered he was an Indian and sneaked to the cave entrance to scout for enemies. Sometimes he let out loud war cries that scared Bill, who was already nervous.
"Red Chief," I said to the boy, "would you like to go home?"
“Aw, why would I want to go home?” he said. “I don’t have any fun there. I hate school. I like camping out. You won’t make me go back home, will you, Snake-eye?”
“Not right away,” I said. “We’ll stay in the cave for a while.”
“Great!” he said. “This is the most fun I’ve ever had.”

We went to bed around eleven o’clock. We laid out some big blankets and quilts and put Red Chief between us. We weren't worried he'd run away. He kept us awake for three hours, jumping up and grabbing his stick rifle, yelling, “Hist! pard,” into our ears whenever he imagined he heard an enemy approaching. Finally, I fell into a restless sleep and dreamed I had been kidnapped and tied to a tree by a mean pirate with red hair.

At dawn, I was awakened by awful screams from Bill. These weren't normal yells or howls; they were high-pitched, terrifying screams like you'd hear from someone who just saw a ghost. Hearing a big, tough man scream that way in a cave at dawn is chilling.

I jumped up to see what was happening. Red Chief was sitting on Bill’s chest with one hand tangled in his hair. In the other hand, he held the sharp knife we used to cut bacon and was trying to scalp Bill, just like he had said he would the night before.

I took the knife from the boy and made him lie down again. But from that moment, Bill was broken. He lay on his side of the bed but didn't close his eyes to sleep as long as that boy was with us. I dozed off for a bit, but as the sun started to rise, I remembered that Red Chief had said he would burn me at the stake at sunrise. I wasn’t scared, but I sat up, lit my pipe, and leaned against a rock.
“Why are you up so early, Sam?” Bill asked.
“Me?” I said. “Oh, I just have a bit of pain in my shoulder. I thought sitting up might help.”

“You’re lying!” Bill said. “You’re scared. You were supposed to be burned at sunrise, and you were afraid he’d actually do it. And he would if he could find a match. Isn’t it awful, Sam? Do you think anyone will pay money to get a little troublemaker like that back home?”
“Sure,” I said. “Parents usually love kids like that the most. Now, you and Red Chief cook breakfast while I go up the mountain and look around.”

I went up to the top of the little mountain and scanned the area. I thought I might see the villagers from Summit searching for us with farm tools. Instead, everything was peaceful. I saw a man plowing with a mule, but no one seemed to be looking for a kidnapped boy. Everything seemed calm and sleepy.
“Maybe no one has noticed that he’s missing yet,” I thought. “Heaven help us when they do!” Then I went back down the mountain to eat breakfast.
When I got to the cave, I saw Bill standing against the wall, breathing hard. The boy was threatening to hit him with a rock the size of a coconut.
“He put a hot boiled potato down my back and then stepped on it,” Bill explained, “so I boxed his ears. Do you have a gun, Sam?”
I took the rock from the boy and tried to calm things down. “I’ll get you for this!” the boy said to Bill. “No one hits Red Chief and gets away with it. You better watch out!”
After breakfast, the boy took a piece of leather with strings from his pocket and went outside the cave, unwinding it.
“What’s he up to now?” Bill asked anxiously. “You don’t think he’ll run away, do you, Sam?”

“No chance,” I said. “He doesn't seem to like being at home. But we need to come up with a plan for the ransom. No one in Summit seems worried about his disappearance yet; maybe they think he's staying with a relative or a neighbor. But they’ll miss him today. Tonight, we need to send a message to his father, demanding two thousand dollars for his return.”
Just then, we heard a loud shout, like a battle cry. The boy had pulled a sling from his pocket and was swinging it around his head.

I ducked and heard a heavy thud and a sigh from Bill, like the sound a horse makes when you take off its saddle. A rock the size of an egg had hit Bill just behind his left ear. He went limp and fell into the fire, landing on the pan of hot water we used for washing dishes. I pulled him out and poured cold water on his head for half an hour.
Eventually, Bill sat up and rubbed behind his ear. “Sam, do you know who my favorite character from the Bible is?” he asked.
“Take it easy,” I said. “You’ll feel better soon.”
“King Herod,” he replied. “You won’t leave me here alone with that kid, will you, Sam?”
I went out and grabbed the boy, shaking him until his freckles rattled.
“If you don’t behave,” I said, “I’ll take you straight home. Are you going to be good or not?”
“I was just having fun,” he said sullenly. “I didn’t mean to hurt Old Hank. But why did he hit me? I’ll be good, Snake-eye, if you won’t send me home and let me play the Black Scout today.”

“I don’t know that game,” I said. “You and Mr. Bill can decide. He’s your playmate for today. I’m going away for a while on business. Now go in and apologize to him, or you’re going home immediately.”

I made the boy and Bill shake hands, then took Bill aside and told him I was going to Poplar Cove, a small village three miles from the cave, to find out how people in Summit were reacting to the kidnapping. I also planned to send a letter to old man Dorset demanding the ransom and explaining how to pay it.

“Sam,” Bill said, “I’ve stood by you through everything—earthquakes, fires, floods, poker games, dynamite explosions, police raids, train robberies, and cyclones. I never lost my nerve until we kidnapped this kid. He’s driving me crazy. You won’t leave me alone with him for long, will you, Sam?”
“I’ll be back this afternoon,” I said. “You need to keep the boy entertained and quiet until I get back. Now let's write the ransom letter to old Dorset.”

Bill and I got some paper and a pencil and started writing the ransom letter. Meanwhile, Red Chief, wrapped in a blanket, was patrolling back and forth, guarding the cave entrance. Bill was practically begging me to lower the ransom to fifteen hundred dollars instead of two thousand. “I’m not trying to undermine the strong love parents have for their kids,” he said, “but we’re dealing with humans here. No one’s going to pay two thousand dollars for that wild, freckled kid. I’m okay with taking a chance at fifteen hundred. I’ll cover the difference.”
To ease Bill’s worries, I agreed, and we wrote the letter together. It went like this:
Ebenezer Dorset, Esq.:

We have your boy hidden far from Summit. It’s useless for you or any detectives to try to find him. The only way you’ll get him back is by following these terms: We demand fifteen hundred dollars in large bills for his return. Leave the money at midnight tonight at the same spot and in the same box as your reply, which we will describe. If you agree to these terms, send your answer by a single messenger tonight at half-past eight.

After crossing Owl Creek on the road to Poplar Cove, look for three large trees about a hundred yards apart, next to the wheat field fence on the right. At the bottom of the fence post by the third tree, you’ll find a small pasteboard box.
The messenger will place the answer in this box and then return immediately to Summit.
If you attempt any tricks or fail to meet our demand, you’ll never see your boy again.
If you pay the money, he will be returned to you safe and sound within three hours. These terms are final, and if you don’t agree, there will be no further communication.
TWO DESPERATE MEN.
I addressed the letter to Dorset and put it in my pocket. Just as I was about to leave, the kid came up to me and said, “Aw, Snake-eye, you said I could play the Black Scout while you were gone.”
“Of course, you can play,” I said. “Mr. Bill will play with you. What kind of game is it?”
“I’m the Black Scout,” says Red Chief, “and I have to ride to the stockade to warn the settlers that the Indians are coming. I’m tired of playing Indian myself. I want to be the Black Scout.”
“All right,” I said. “That sounds harmless enough. I guess Mr. Bill will help you out.”
“What do I have to do?” Bill asked warily, eyeing the kid.
“You’re the horse,” said the Black Scout. “Get down on your hands and knees. How can I ride to the stockade without a horse?”
“It might be best to keep him busy,” I said to Bill. “Just play along.”
Bill reluctantly got down on all fours, looking like a trapped rabbit.
“How far is it to the stockade, kid?” he asked in a strained voice.
“Ninety miles,” said the Black Scout. “And you have to hurry to get there on time. Whoa, now!”
The Black Scout jumped on Bill’s back and kicked his heels into Bill’s sides like he was riding a real horse.

“For heaven’s sake,” said Bill, “hurry back, Sam, as soon as you can. I wish we hadn’t asked for more than a thousand dollars. And you, kid, stop kicking me or I’ll get up and give you a good spanking.”

I walked over to Poplar Cove and hung around the post office and store, chatting with the locals. One old man mentioned that Summit was in an uproar because Elder Ebenezer Dorset’s boy was missing. That’s all I needed to know. I bought some smoking tobacco, casually asked about the price of black-eyed peas, secretly posted my letter, and left. The postmaster said the mail carrier would come in an hour to take the mail to Summit.
When I got back to the cave, Bill and the boy were gone. I searched around and called out, but there was no reply. So, I lit my pipe and sat down on a mossy bank to wait.

After about thirty minutes, I heard the bushes rustle. Bill stumbled out into the clearing, looking exhausted. Behind him was the kid, grinning widely and moving stealthily like a scout. Bill stopped, took off his hat, and wiped his face with a red handkerchief. The boy stood about eight feet behind him.

“Sam,” Bill said, “you’re probably going to think I’m a coward, but I couldn’t help it. I’m an adult man, capable of defending myself, but there’s a limit to what a person can take. The boy is gone. I sent him home. It’s over. There have been martyrs in the past who suffered greatly for their beliefs, but none of them endured the tortures I’ve been through. I tried to stick to our plan, but I reached my breaking point.”
“What’s the trouble, Bill?” I asked.

“I was ridden the full ninety miles to the stockade,” Bill said, “without a break. Then, when the settlers were ‘rescued,’ I was given oats to eat, which turned out to be sand. And then, for an hour, the kid asked me endless questions—why there’s nothing in holes, how a road can go both ways, and why the grass is green. A person can only take so much, Sam. I took him by the scruff of his neck and dragged him down the mountain. He kicked my legs black-and-blue from the knees down and bit my thumb and hand. I need to get those bites treated.

“But he’s gone,” Bill continued. “Gone home. I showed him the way back to Summit and gave him a good kick to send him on his way. I’m sorry we won’t get the ransom, but it was either that or Bill Driscoll ending up in a madhouse.”
Two Desperate Men,

Gentlemen: I received your letter today regarding the ransom for my son. I think your demands are a bit high, so I have a counter-proposal that I believe you will accept. Bring Johnny home and pay me $250 in cash, and I agree to take him off your hands. It’s best you come at night, as the neighbors think he’s lost and I can’t guarantee their actions if they see you bringing him back.
Very respectfully,
EBENEZER DORSET
“Great pirates of Penzance!” I exclaimed. “Of all the nerve—”
But I looked at Bill and hesitated. He had the most desperate look in his eyes that I had ever seen.

“Sam,” he said, “what’s $250, anyway? We’ve got the money. One more night with this kid will drive me insane. Plus, Mr. Dorset is being quite generous with his offer. You’re not going to pass this up, are you?”
“To tell you the truth, Bill,” I said, “this kid has gotten on my nerves too. Let’s take him home, pay the ransom, and get out of here.”
That night, we told the boy that his father had bought him a silver rifle and a pair of moccasins, and we were going to hunt bears the next day. That got him to come along without much fuss.
At midnight, we knocked on Ebenezer’s front door. Instead of collecting $1,500 under the tree as per our original plan, Bill was counting out $250 into Dorset’s hand.
When the boy realized we were leaving him at home, he started howling like a siren and clung to Bill’s leg like a leech. His father peeled him off slowly, like removing a sticky plaster.
“How long can you hold him?” Bill asked.
“I’m not as strong as I used to be,” said old Dorset, “but I can promise you ten minutes.”
“That’s enough,” said Bill. “In ten minutes, I’ll be across the Central, Southern, and Midwestern States, heading for the Canadian border.”
As dark as it was and as chubby as Bill was, and as good a runner as I am, Bill was a mile and a half away from Summit before I could catch up to him.

It's true—I have been very, very nervous, but why do you think I am crazy? My illness made my senses sharper—not destroyed. I could hear everything—in heaven, on earth, and even in hell. How can I be mad? Listen, and see how calmly I can tell you the whole story.

I don't know how the idea first came into my mind, but once it was there, it wouldn't leave me alone. I had no reason to do it. I didn't hate the old man. I even loved him. He never hurt me or insulted me. I didn't want his money either. I think it was his eye! Yes, that was it! He had an eye like a vulture—a pale blue eye with a film over it. Whenever it looked at me, I felt cold inside. Slowly, I decided to kill the old man and get rid of that eye forever.

Now, you might think I'm crazy. But mad people don't plan things carefully. You should have seen me. I was very careful and smart about it! I was never nicer to the old man than the week before I killed him. Every night, at midnight, I would slowly open his door. Oh, so gently! I made a small opening for my head. I had a lantern that was closed so no light shone out. I put my head in, very slowly, so I wouldn't wake the old man. It took me an hour to get my whole head in so I could see him on his bed. Would a madman be this careful? After getting my head in, I opened the lantern just a tiny bit so a small ray of light fell on his vulture eye. I did this for seven nights, but his eye was always closed, so I couldn't do it. It wasn't the old man who bothered me, but his Evil Eye. Every morning, I spoke to him cheerfully, asking how he slept. He never suspected that every night, at midnight, I watched him while he slept.

On the eighth night, I was even more careful opening the door. I felt powerful and proud. I was opening the door slowly, and he didn't know my secret. I almost laughed at the idea, but he moved in bed suddenly. Maybe he heard me. But I didn't stop. His room was very dark because the shutters were closed. I knew he couldn't see me. I kept pushing the door open slowly.
I had my head in and was about to open the lantern when my thumb slipped, and the old man sat up in bed, shouting, “Who’s there?”

I stayed very still and said nothing. For an hour, I didn't move. The old man didn't lie back down. He was sitting up in bed, listening. Then I heard him groan—a sound of mortal terror. It wasn't pain or sorrow, but a deep fear. I knew that sound well. Many nights at midnight, when all slept, I felt that fear too. I understood how the old man felt and felt sorry for him, though I was still planning to kill him. He was trying to make himself believe the noise was nothing—a wind in the chimney, a mouse crossing the floor, or a cricket. But it was useless. Death was coming for him, and he felt my presence in the room.

After waiting a long time, while he still sat up, I decided to open the lantern a little bit, very carefully. Finally, a thin ray of light, like a spider’s thread, shone out and fell on his vulture eye.

The old man's eye was wide open—very wide—and it made me so angry. I could see it clearly, a dull blue with a creepy film over it. It sent chills down my spine. I couldn’t look at anything else of the old man, because I had the light shining exactly on that terrible eye.

Let me remind you, what you think is madness in me is actually sharp senses. I began to hear a low, quick sound, like a watch ticking under a cushion. I recognized that sound—it was the old man's heart beating. It made me even angrier, like a drumbeat making a soldier brave.

But I stayed still. I barely breathed. I kept the lantern steady, focusing the light on his eye. The sound of his heart got louder and quicker. The old man must have been terrified! The sound kept growing, louder and louder. Do you understand how nervous I was? In the dead silence of that old house, the noise drove me to uncontrollable fear. Still, I waited, standing still. But the heart's beating grew louder and louder. I thought it would burst. Then I started worrying that the sound would be heard by a neighbor. The old man’s time had come! With a loud yell, I opened the lantern fully and jumped into the room. He screamed once—just once. Quickly, I pulled him to the floor and pushed the heavy bed over him. I smiled because the job was almost done. But his heart kept beating for a while, though muffled. This didn’t bother me; it wouldn’t be heard through the walls. Finally, it stopped. The old man was dead. I checked his body under the bed. Yes, he was totally dead. I touched his heart to make sure it wasn’t beating. He was definitely dead. His eye would bother me no more.

If you still think I’m mad, you won’t after I tell you how carefully I hid the body. It was still dark as midnight, and I worked quickly but quietly. First, I cut off his head, arms, and legs. I then pulled up three floorboards and placed the body parts inside. I put the boards back so perfectly that no one could tell they had been moved. There was no mess, not a single blood spot. I had caught all the blood in a tub. Ha ha!

By the time I finished, it was four o'clock, still dark. As the clock struck the hour, there was a knock at the door. I went down with a light heart, for I had nothing to fear now. Three men came in—they were police officers. They said a neighbor had heard a scream and reported it, so they needed to search the house.

I smiled because I had nothing to worry about. I welcomed them. I told them the scream was my own from a dream. I said the old man was away in the country. I showed them around the house, telling them to look everywhere. Finally, I took them to his bedroom. I showed them his undisturbed treasures. Feeling proud and confident, I brought chairs for them and invited them to rest. In my triumph, I placed my own chair right over the spot where the old man's body was hidden.

The officers were convinced. My calm manner put them at ease. They sat down, and we started chatting about ordinary things. But soon, I felt uneasy. My head hurt, and I thought I heard a ringing in my ears. The officers stayed and kept talking. The ringing sound got louder and more clear. I tried talking more to get rid of it, but the noise didn’t go away. It got louder and louder until I realized it wasn’t just in my ears.

I must have turned very pale, but I kept talking even louder. The sound was a low, quick beat, like a watch ticking under a cushion. I struggled to breathe, but the officers didn’t seem to hear it. I talked faster and more intensely, but the noise kept growing. I stood up and started arguing about little things, waving my hands around, but it didn’t help. The noise grew louder. Why wouldn’t the officers leave? I paced the floor, heavy footsteps, pretending to be upset by what they said, but the noise still grew.

Oh God, what could I do? I was desperate. I shouted, I raved, I cursed. I picked up the chair I was sitting on and rubbed it against the floor, but the sound rose above everything. It got louder—louder—louder! And still, the officers chatted and smiled. Couldn’t they hear it? Almighty God, they must have heard it! They knew! They were mocking me! Anything was better than this torture. I couldn’t stand their fake smiles any longer. I felt I would scream or die.
The noise grew louder and louder. “Villains!” I screamed, “Stop pretending! I admit it! Tear up the planks! Here, here! It’s the beating of his hideous heart!”

chapters.title: THE FALL OF THE HOUSE OF USHER - Edgar Allan Poe

paragraphs.content: On a very gloomy and quiet day in autumn, when the clouds were very low, I was riding my horse alone through a very sad area. As evening came, I could see the sad House of Usher. I don't know why, but when I saw the house, I felt very gloomy. This gloom was deep; it wasn't like the usual sad but interesting feelings that nature can give. I looked at the house, the walls, the empty windows, some tall grass, and some old tree trunks, and felt very sad. It felt like waking up from a dream after taking opium - a sharp return to reality. I felt cold, downhearted, and my thoughts were very gloomy. I couldn't make these thoughts feel uplifting. I wondered why this house made me feel so nervous. It was a mystery I couldn't solve. I thought maybe it was just the way things were arranged here that made me feel so sad. Thinking this, I stopped my horse near the edge of a dark, still lake next to the house and looked into the water. I saw the reflection of the grass, the trees, and the windows, and I shuddered even more than before.

I planned to stay a few weeks in this dark and gloomy house. The owner, Roderick Usher, was a close friend when we were young, but it had been many years since we last saw each other. Recently, I received a letter from him while I was far away. The letter showed he was very upset and needed to see me in person. He wrote about being very sick both in body and mind, and he really wanted to see me, his best and only close friend, hoping that my company could make him feel a bit better. The way he wrote the letter and how sincere he seemed made me decide to go right away, even though it was a very strange request.

Even though we were close friends as boys, I didn't know much about my friend because he was very private. I knew that his family was very old and famous for being sensitive, which showed in their art and quiet charity work. They loved music deeply, especially the complex parts. I also found out that his family had always been direct descendants without branching out much. I thought about how the family's simple lineage and their home might have influenced each other over the years. This lack of side branches in the family, and the passing of the family wealth and name directly from father to son, might be why the family and their home were so closely linked that people started calling their estate 'the House of Usher,' a name that referred to both the family and their home.

I've mentioned that my simple test of looking into the water only made my strange feeling stronger. I believe that being aware of my growing fear only made it increase faster. This often happens with feelings based on fear. Perhaps this is why, when I looked up from the water to the house again, I started to think a weird thought. It was a silly idea, but I mention it to show how strong my feelings were. I had convinced myself that there was a special atmosphere around the big house and its grounds, different from the normal air. It seemed to come from the old trees, the gray walls, and the quiet water. It was a heavy and mysterious mist, slow, barely visible, and gray.

Waking up from what seemed like a dream, I looked closely at the old building. It looked very old because of its color and small fungi covered the outside like a thin net. Even though it was old, it wasn't falling apart. The building still fit together well, but the stones themselves were crumbling. This reminded me of old wood that has rotted for years in a place with no fresh air. Besides showing signs of being very old, the building didn't seem like it was going to fall down. If someone looked very closely, they might see a small crack starting at the roof and going down the wall in a zigzag pattern until it disappeared into the dark water of the lake.

I noticed some things as I rode across a small bridge to the house. A servant took my horse and I walked through a big, old-fashioned entrance into the house. A quiet valet led me silently through many dark, twisting hallways to his master's art studio. Along the way, everything I saw made me feel even more uneasy, though I couldn't explain why. Even though I was used to seeing old decorations and dark walls from when I was young, the usual things seemed to stir strange thoughts in me. On one of the staircases, I met the family's doctor. He looked both sneaky and confused. He seemed nervous when he talked to me and then he walked away. The valet opened a door and I went into the room where his master was. The room was very big and tall. The windows were long and thin, and so high up from the dark wooden floor that you couldn't reach them. Dim red light came through the windows, showing the main things in the room, but I couldn't see the corners or the detailed ceiling. Dark curtains were on the walls. The room was filled with old, worn-out furniture, lots of books, and musical instruments, but it all felt sad and lifeless. The whole place felt very gloomy and sad.

When I entered, Usher got up from a sofa where he was lying down and warmly welcomed me. At first, I thought he was being too friendly in a forced way, like someone who is bored of the world. But when I looked at his face, I could see he was truly sincere. We sat down and I looked at him quietly, feeling both pity and awe. I was shocked at how much Roderick Usher had changed in such a short time! It was hard for me to believe that this pale person was the same friend from my childhood. His face had always been unique. He had a very pale and thin face, bright and big eyes, thin lips with a beautiful shape, a delicate nose, and a small chin that suggested he was not very energetic. His hair was very soft and thin. His face, with its wide forehead, was memorable. Now, his features looked even more extreme, and this made him look so different that I wasn't sure who I was talking to. His skin was very pale, and his eyes shone strangely, which surprised and scared me. His silky hair had grown wildly and floated around his face, making him look less like a regular person.

When I met my friend, I noticed he was very nervous and inconsistent. He seemed to be struggling with his nerves. I was expecting this a bit because of his past behavior and his physical and emotional nature that I knew from his letter. His actions and voice changed quickly from being lively to being very quiet. His voice would sometimes shake and then suddenly become strong and clear. He talked about why I wanted to visit him, how much he wanted to see me, and how he thought I could make him feel better. He talked a lot about what he thought was wrong with him, which he believed was a problem that ran in his family and had no cure. He called it a nervous problem that he hoped would go away soon. He felt many strange feelings. Some of what he told me was confusing and interesting. He was very sensitive; simple food was all he could eat, he could only wear clothes made of certain materials, he couldn't stand the smell of flowers, even dim light hurt his eyes, and only some music from string instruments didn't scare him.

He was extremely afraid and believed he would die because of his overwhelming fear. He said, 'I will die from this terrible fear. I am scared not by what happens, but by what could happen because of these events. I am terrified by even the smallest thing that could make my fear worse. I am not scared of danger itself, but of the fear it causes. In my weak and sad state, I know that soon I will lose both my life and my sanity in a battle with my fear.' Additionally, I learned that he had strange beliefs about the house he lived in. He thought the house had a special power over him because of its shape and the old, gray walls and towers. He had not left this house for many years because of these beliefs.

He admitted, though hesitantly, that his deep sadness was mostly because of his sister's long and serious illness. She was very close to dying, and she was his only family and had been his companion for many years. He said sadly that her death would leave him as the last member of his family, the Ushers. While he was talking, his sister, Madeline, walked through the room. She didn't see me and then she left. I was very surprised and a bit scared when I saw her, but I couldn't explain why I felt that way. I felt numb as I watched her leave. When she was gone, I looked at her brother. He was covering his face with his hands, crying, and looked very pale. Madeline's illness was very strange and the doctors couldn't cure her. She was very weak, didn't show much emotion, and sometimes seemed frozen. She had been fighting her illness, but the night I arrived, she became too weak and stayed in bed. Her brother was very upset when he told me this. I realized that the short time I saw her might be the last, and I might not see her alive again.

For many days, we did not talk about her name, Usher or I. During this time, I tried hard to make my friend feel better. We spent time painting and reading together. Sometimes, I would just listen while he played his guitar in a unique way. As we became closer friends, I saw more of his deep feelings. I realized it was very hard to make him feel happier because he always seemed to be surrounded by sadness that affected everything around him.

I will always remember the many serious times I spent alone with the owner of the House of Usher. However, I can't fully describe the types of studies or activities he got me involved in. His intense and troubled imagination made everything seem mysterious. I will never forget his long, sad songs. I also clearly remember a strange twist he added to a famous waltz by Von Weber. The paintings he worked on, which became more unclear the more he worked on them, scared me in a way I couldn't understand. I can't fully explain these paintings with just words. His simple and direct style in these paintings really captured and held my attention. If anyone ever painted a true idea, it was Roderick Usher. For me, during that time, looking at his paintings filled me with a deep fear I had never felt before, unlike looking at the more realistic and vivid paintings of Fuseli.

One of my friend's imaginative ideas, which is not too abstract, can be weakly described using words. A small image showed the inside of a very long and straight tunnel or vault with low, smooth, white walls that had no breaks or decorations. Some extra details in the picture made it clear that this tunnel was very deep underground. There was no exit seen anywhere in the tunnel, and there was no torch or any other man-made light source visible; however, a strong light filled the area, giving it a strange and eerie brightness.

I mentioned earlier that there was a person who found all music painful to listen to, except for some sounds from string instruments. He mostly played the guitar, and this is probably why his music sounded so unusual. His ability to create music spontaneously was impressive and came from a very focused state of mind, which he reached during moments of intense excitement. I remember the words of one of his songs very clearly because it made a strong impression on me. He seemed to understand for the first time that his mind was becoming unstable. The song was called 'The Haunted Palace,' and it went something like this:

I. In the greenest valley, where good angels lived, there once stood a beautiful and grand palace that shone brightly. It was in a place ruled by Thought, and it was so beautiful that no angel could compare.
II. There were golden flags on the roof that moved in the breeze (this was a long time ago). And every soft breeze that passed brought a pleasant scent along the decorated walls.

III. People walking in this happy valley could see, through two bright windows, spirits moving in time to the music of a lute. Near a throne, where a great ruler sat, everyone could see his majesty fitting for his position.
IV. The beautiful palace door shone with pearls and rubies. Through it came a group of Echoes, singing beautifully, only to praise their king's cleverness and wisdom.

V. But sad, evil things attacked the king's great position; (We should feel sad, because he will be alone and no new day will brighten for him!), and the beauty that was once around his home is now just a faint story from a long-gone past.

VI. Now, travelers in that valley see huge shapes moving strangely through windows glowing red, to a clashing music; while something like a fast and scary river of terrible figures rushes out the pale door, laughing loudly but never smiling.

I clearly remember how this song made us think deeply and led us to discuss a belief Usher had. He strongly believed that all plants are aware in some way. In his troubled mind, this idea went further, suggesting that even non-living things could have life under certain conditions. I find it hard to fully describe how strongly he believed this. He connected this belief with the old stones of his family's home. He thought that the way these stones were arranged, along with the mushrooms growing on them and the old trees standing nearby, all contributed to this awareness. He believed that the long time these things had been this way, and their reflection in the lake, proved his point. He claimed that this awareness created a special atmosphere around the lake and the house. He thought this influence had shaped his family's history for centuries and affected him directly. I won't add my own thoughts on these unusual ideas.

Our books, which were very important to someone who was often ill, matched the strange and ghostly nature of his thoughts. We often read together books like 'Ververt et Chartreuse' by Gresset and 'Belphegor' by Machiavelli. We also read 'Heaven and Hell' by Swedenborg, and many other unusual and mysterious books. One book he liked a lot was called 'Directorium Inquisitorium' by Eymeric de Gironne. He also enjoyed a very old and special book called 'Vigiliae Mortuorum secundum Chorum Ecclesiae Maguntinae', which was about church practices.

I couldn't stop thinking about how strange this work was, and how it might affect the sad man, when one night he suddenly told me that lady Madeline had died. He planned to keep her body in a vault inside the building for two weeks before burying her. The reason he gave for doing this was something I couldn't argue against. He decided to do this because of the unusual illness she had, the doctors' nosy questions, and because their family's burial ground was far away and isolated. I must admit, remembering the creepy face of the person I saw on the stairs when I first came to the house, I didn't want to argue against what seemed like a safe and not too strange thing to do.

Usher asked for my help to prepare for a temporary burial. After putting the body in a coffin, just the two of us carried it to its final place. The vault where we placed it was small, damp, and dark, with no windows, and was located deep under the house, right below my bedroom. It seemed like in the old times, this vault was used for dark purposes and later as a storage for explosive materials, because its floor and the inside of a long hallway leading to it were covered with copper. The door was made of heavy iron and also covered with copper. It made a loud scraping noise when it opened or closed.

We placed our sad burden on supports in this scary place, and opened the coffin lid a little to see the face inside. I noticed for the first time how much the brother and sister looked alike. Usher, guessing what I was thinking, whispered a few words telling me that the person who had died was his twin, and that they had always shared a special connection. We didn't look at the dead body for long because it was unsettling. The illness that caused her death at a young age left a slight blush on her face and chest, and a creepy smile that sometimes remains after death. We then closed and sealed the coffin, locked the iron door, and went back up to the less dark parts of the house.

After several sad days, I noticed a clear change in my friend's mental state. He no longer acted like his usual self. He forgot or ignored his normal activities. He walked quickly and aimlessly from room to room. His face looked even paler than before, but his eyes no longer shone. His voice, once sometimes hoarse, now shook with what sounded like great fear. Sometimes, I thought he was troubled by a big secret he wanted to share but couldn't find the bravery to do so. Other times, I thought it was just madness, as he would stare at nothing for hours as if he was listening to a sound that wasn't there. His condition was frightening and started to affect me too. I began to feel the strange and strong influence of his unusual beliefs.

It was late at night, about a week after we put lady Madeline in the underground room, when I really felt strong emotions. I couldn't sleep at all as the hours slowly passed. I tried hard to calm my nerves. I tried to think that my feelings were mostly because of the scary-looking room and the dark, old curtains that moved when the wind blew. But nothing helped. I started to shake uncontrollably and felt a deep, unexplained fear. I tried to shake it off, sat up in bed, and looked into the dark room, listening to strange, quiet sounds that came with the storm, from somewhere I didn't know. Feeling extremely scared for no clear reason, I quickly got dressed because I knew I wouldn't sleep anymore that night. I tried to feel better by walking back and forth in the room.

I had just walked a little when I heard someone lightly walking on the stairs next to me. I quickly realized it was Usher. Soon after, he knocked softly on my door and came in with a lamp. His face looked very pale and strangely excited, but I was so tired of being alone that I was happy to see him. 'And you have not seen it?' he asked suddenly, after looking around quietly. 'You haven't seen it? But wait, you will.' Saying this, he covered his lamp a bit and opened a window quickly, letting the storm in. The strong wind almost knocked us over. It was a wild, stormy night, both scary and beautiful. It seemed like a whirlwind was near us because the wind changed direction a lot, and the thick clouds were very low but moved fast across the sky. Even though it was so cloudy, we could see the clouds moving because of a weird light that made everything glow, but we couldn't see the moon, stars, or any lightning.

"You must not see this!" I said to Usher, shaking, as I moved him gently from the window to a chair. "What you see that confuses you are just natural lights, or maybe they come from the bad air over the water. Let's close the window; the air is cold and not good for you. Here is a book you like. I will read, and you will listen; and this way, we will spend this scary night together. The old book I picked up was 'Mad Trist' by Sir Launcelot Canning. I said it was Usher's favorite more as a sad joke than seriously; because, really, there's not much interesting in its boring and unimaginative writing for someone as thoughtful as my friend. But it was the only book nearby; and I hoped that the excitement Usher felt might be eased (as strange things often help in mental illness) even by the silliness of the story I was about to read. Seeing how eagerly he seemed to listen, I thought maybe my plan was working.

I reached the famous part of the story where Ethelred, the hero, couldn't peacefully enter the hermit's home, so he decided to force his way in. The story goes like this: 'Ethelred, who was naturally brave and now even stronger from drinking wine, didn't wait to talk to the hermit, who was stubborn and mean. Feeling the rain on his shoulders and worried about the storm, he lifted his heavy club and smashed the door's wood to make space for his hand in armor. Pulling hard, he broke the wood so loudly that the noise echoed through the forest.'

When I finished that sentence, I suddenly felt startled and stopped for a moment. It seemed to me, though I quickly thought it was just my imagination, that I heard a faint sound from a far part of the house. It sounded like the noise Sir Launcelot had described, but it was very muted. I was sure it was just a coincidence that made me notice it because there were other sounds from the storm and the windows. These sounds were not supposed to interest or bother me. I went on with the story: 'But the brave knight Ethelred, who just entered the door, was very surprised and angry to see no trace of the evil hermit. Instead, there was a huge, scaly dragon with a fiery tongue, guarding a golden palace with silver floors. On the wall, there was a shiny brass shield with a message: 'Who enters here, a conqueror has been; Who kills the dragon, wins the shield.'

Ethelred lifted his weapon and hit the dragon on the head. The dragon fell and died, screaming so loudly and terribly that Ethelred had to cover his ears with his hands because of the awful noise, which was like nothing ever heard before. I stopped reading suddenly, feeling very surprised. I was sure I heard a strange, long, and harsh sound, although I couldn't tell where it was coming from. It sounded just like the dragon's scream in the story.

Feeling overwhelmed by a surprising event that caused both amazement and fear, I managed to stay calm enough not to upset my friend, who seemed very tense. I wasn't sure if he heard the strange sounds, but I noticed a change in his behavior recently. He moved his chair to face the door, and I could only see part of his face. His lips were moving silently, and his head was bowed, though his eyes were open, showing he wasn't asleep. He was also swaying gently side to side. Noticing all this, I continued telling the story of Sir Launcelot, who had just escaped a dragon. Sir Launcelot thought of a magical shield and moved the dragon's body out of his way. As he walked bravely across the castle's floor, the shield fell from the wall to the ground with a loud clang.

As soon as I spoke, I heard a loud, echoing sound like metal hitting metal. It scared me, and I jumped up. Usher, however, kept sitting calmly and rocking. I ran to him and saw he was staring straight ahead with a frozen look on his face. When I touched his shoulder, he shivered all over, gave a weak smile, and started mumbling quickly in a low voice, not noticing I was there. I leaned in closer and finally understood the terrible meaning of what he was saying.

Can't you hear it? Yes, I hear it, and I've been hearing it for a long time. For many minutes, hours, days, I've been hearing it but I was too scared to speak. We accidentally buried her alive! Didn't I tell you I have very sharp senses? I'm telling you now, I heard her weak movements inside the coffin a long time ago, but I was too scared to say anything. And tonight, as the story goes, with the loud noises and the dragon's scream, it was actually the sound of her coffin breaking and the metal hinges creaking as she struggled! Where can I go now? Won't she come soon to blame me for being so quick? Didn't I hear her footsteps? Can't I hear the terrible pounding of her heart? I'm not crazy! She is right outside the door now!

I ran away from that house, terrified. As I crossed the old bridge, the storm was still raging. Suddenly, a strange light appeared on the path, and I looked back to see where it came from. Only the big house and its shadows were behind me. The light was from the bright, red moon that was setting, shining through a crack in the house I had mentioned earlier. This crack had started from the roof and went down in a zigzag pattern. As I watched, the crack got bigger quickly. A strong wind blew, and suddenly I could see the whole moon. The walls of the house fell apart. I heard a loud noise like many waters. Then, the dark lake at my feet quietly covered up the broken pieces of the 'House of Usher.'

chapters.title: BOULE DE SUIF - Guy de Maupassant

paragraphs.content: For many days, scattered groups of defeated soldiers had been moving through the town. These were not organized soldiers but rather a messy crowd, looking dirty and tired, their clothes worn and torn. They walked without any flags or order, very tired and unable to think clearly, just marching because they were used to it, and they would collapse from exhaustion as soon as they stopped walking. Most of these soldiers were from the militia, normal people not used to fighting, weighed down by their heavy gear; they were easily scared but also easily excited, quick to fight but also quick to run away. Among them were a few soldiers in red pants, survivors from a big battle, and some artillerymen in dark coats, mixed with other infantrymen. Sometimes, there was also a soldier in a shiny helmet and heavy boots, struggling to keep up with the faster infantry soldiers.
Groups of volunteer fighters, with names like 'Avengers of the Defeat,' 'Citizens of the Tomb,' and 'Companies in Death,' also passed by, looking more like a gang of bandits than soldiers.

Their leaders, who used to be shopkeepers or traders and had become officers because they had money or long moustaches, were covered in weapons and fancy clothes. They spoke loudly about battle plans and acted as if they were the only hope for France in her tough times. However, they were often scared of their own men, who were reckless and liked to loot and cause trouble.

The report said that the Prussians were going to enter Rouen soon. The National Guard, who had been very careful in checking the nearby woods for two months, sometimes even shooting their own guards by mistake and getting ready to fight if a rabbit moved in the bushes, had now gone back home. They put away their weapons, uniforms, and all the scary equipment they used to make people afraid for miles around, and it was all gone. Finally, the last of the French soldiers crossed the Seine river heading towards Pont-Audemer through Saint Sever and Bourg-Achard; and then, the very last to leave was their sad general walking on foot with two helpers, unable to do anything with his scattered troops, feeling very confused and shocked because his people, who were used to winning, had lost badly even though they were brave. After that, the city became very quiet, filled with a scared silence. Many fat businessmen, who only knew about trading, were nervously waiting for the winners, worried that their kitchen knives might be seen as weapons. Life seemed to stop, stores were shut, and the streets were empty. Occasionally, a scared person would quickly walk by, staying close to the walls. During this scary waiting time, people really wanted the enemy to arrive.

In the afternoon after the French soldiers left, some Uhlans, who came from nowhere, quickly passed through the city. Soon after, a large group moved towards the city from Sainte-Catherine, and two other groups came from the roads of Darnetal and Bois-guillaume. The leading soldiers of these three groups met at the same time in the town square, while many groups of the German army entered nearby streets, their marching making loud noises on the pavement. Commands in a harsh, unfamiliar language were called out and echoed by the silent, empty-looking houses. People hiding behind closed shutters watched the new powerful rulers of the city. The residents, stuck in their dark homes, felt confused and powerless, like people feel during natural disasters which are too strong to stop. This happens whenever normal life is disrupted, safety is gone, and everything protected by laws is at risk due to uncontrollable forces. Examples like an earthquake destroying a town, a flood sweeping away a farmer, his animals, and parts of his house, or an invading army killing and capturing people, looting and causing destruction while believing they are justified, show how such terrible events make people doubt justice and the protection we expect from higher powers and human reasoning.
Small groups started knocking on doors and then going inside the houses. This happened after one country took over another. Now, the people who lost had to be nice to the people who won.

After some time, when the initial alarms had calmed down, people started feeling peaceful again. In many homes, a Prussian officer would join the family for meals. Often, he was polite and would say he felt sorry for France and didn't like being in the war. The families appreciated his kindness and thought it might be useful to have his protection someday. By being friendly with him, they might have to provide less food for other soldiers. It made no sense to upset someone they relied on so much. This wasn't about being brave, it was about being smart, which the people of Rouen understood well from their past. They believed it was okay to be friendly in private as long as they didn't show it publicly. On the streets, they acted as if they didn't know each other, but at home, they were quite friendly, and the German officer often stayed late into the evening by the fire. The town slowly started to look normal again. The local French people didn't go out much, but Prussian soldiers were everywhere. The blue-uniformed hussar officers, looking very proud with their weapons, didn't seem to look down on the townspeople more than the French officers from before. Still, there was a strange feeling everywhere, like a bad smell that you can't find. This feeling made everything seem different, as if the food tasted weird and made it feel like being far from home, in a place with strange and possibly dangerous people.

The conquerors asked for a lot of money. The local people paid them because they were rich. However, the richer a shopkeeper from Normandy gets, the more it hurts him to lose any amount of his money to someone else. A few miles outside the town, by the river near places like Croisset, Dieppedalle, or Biessard, sailors and fishermen sometimes found dead German soldiers in the water. These soldiers had been killed secretly, perhaps with a knife, a kick, or hit by a stone, and then thrown from a bridge. The river mud hid many acts of revenge and silent brave actions that were more dangerous for the person doing them than fighting in a battle during the day without any recognition. People hate invaders enough that some are willing to risk their lives for their beliefs. Eventually, when the local shopkeepers saw that the invaders controlled the city but did not do the terrible things people thought they would, they began to feel hopeful and started thinking about business again. Some of them needed to go to Havre, a place controlled by the French army, and planned to travel by land to Dieppe to catch a boat. They used their connections with German officers to get a pass from the general. So, they arranged a large carriage pulled by four horses, and ten people signed up to travel. They decided to leave early on Tuesday morning to avoid attracting attention.

For several days, the ground was frozen because it was very cold. On Monday afternoon, around three o'clock, big dark clouds from the north brought snow. It snowed all evening and all night without stopping. At half past four, travelers gathered in the courtyard of the Hotel de Normandie, ready to leave. They were all very cold and half asleep, wrapped in thick clothes. It was so dark that they looked like big, round priests in long coats. Two men recognized each other and started talking with a third man. One said he was taking his wife with him, and the others said they were doing the same. They all planned not to go back to Rouen and would escape to England if enemy soldiers came to Havre. Meanwhile, there was no sign of the horses being ready. A small lantern appeared and disappeared in the darkness as a stable worker moved around. The sound of horses' hooves could be heard, muffled by the straw on the ground. A man was talking and swearing at the horses in the stable. The faint sound of bells indicated that the horses were being harnessed, getting louder and then stopping as the horses moved.

A door shut quickly and everything went quiet. The travelers stopped talking and did not move. It kept snowing heavily, covering everything in a layer of white. The town was very quiet under the snow. All you could hear was the soft sound of snow falling, which felt more like a feeling than a sound. The man came back with a lantern and a sad horse that didn't want to move. He connected the horse to the carriage and spent a lot of time fixing the harness with only one hand because he was holding the lantern with the other. When he went to get another horse, he saw the travelers standing still and covered in snow. He asked them why they weren't sitting inside the carriage to stay dry. They hadn't thought of that and quickly moved inside. The men helped their wives get in first, then they entered, followed by others who quietly took the remaining seats. The bottom of the carriage was filled with straw that sank under their feet. The women at the end had brought small heaters and started them up, talking quietly about how useful they were. Finally, the carriage, now pulled by six horses instead of four, was ready to go because the road was tough. Someone outside asked if everyone was there. Someone inside said 'Yes,' and they set off.

The vehicle moved very slowly through the snow, making creaking sounds. The horses struggled, breathing heavily, and the driver kept cracking his big whip, which moved like a snake and hit the horses, making them try harder. As the day began, the snow stopped falling, and a dim light shone through the clouds, making the snowy landscape look brighter. Trees covered in ice and snow-covered cottages could be seen. In this dim morning light, the people inside the vehicle started looking at each other with interest. At the back, in the best seats, were Madame and Monsieur Loiseau, who sold wine. Monsieur Loiseau used to work for a boss who lost his business, but Loiseau took over the stock and made a lot of money by selling cheap, low-quality wine to small shops. He was known among his friends as a very cunning and funny man. His reputation for being tricky was so famous that one evening, at a government office, Monsieur Tournel, a smart and sharp man known for his writings, made a joke involving Loiseau's name that made everyone in town laugh for a month.

Loiseau was known for making people laugh with his jokes, good and bad. Everyone who talked about him would say, 'That Loiseau is really something special!' He was not too tall, had a big belly, and a red face with grey whiskers. His wife was tall and strong, with a loud voice and quick to make decisions. She took care of the serious side of their business, while he made everything more fun. Next to them sat Monsieur Carré-Lamadon, a very important man who knew a lot about cotton. He owned three businesses, had a special honor from the government, and was part of the General Council. During the time of the Empire, he was part of a friendly group that disagreed with the government, hoping to improve things later. His wife, Madame Carré-Lamadon, was much younger than him and was very kind to the military officers in Rouen. She was sitting across from her husband, looking very elegant and sad about the poor state of the vehicle they were in. Their neighbors, the Count and Countess Hubert de Breville, came from a very old and respected family in Normandy. The Count, who was older, tried very hard to look like Henri Quatre, a famous king said to have favored an ancestor of his, which made her husband a Count and Governor.

A colleague of Monsieur Carré-Lamadon in the General Council, Count Hubert was part of the Orleanist group in the area. The story of how he married a tradesman's daughter from Nantes was always a secret. However, because the Countess acted very elegantly, was great at hosting, and was rumored to have been loved by a prince, the local nobles respected her. Her home was the most important in the area and kept up old traditions of politeness and was hard to get into. The Brevilles, who had all their money in government bonds, were said to make five hundred thousand francs a year. The six passengers sat at the front of the carriage, they were rich and calm, knowing they were strong—good, wealthy people who were religious and had strong morals. By chance, all the women sat on one side, with the Countess sitting next to two nuns who were busy with their prayer beads and prayers. One nun was old and her face was marked by illness, looking like she had been hurt by small shots; the other was very thin and weak, with a sickly face and a faith that was intense. Opposite the two nuns, there was a man and woman who caught a lot of attention.

The man known as Cornudet, also called 'the demon,' was feared by all who followed the rules. For twenty years, he was seen drinking beer at cafes that liked democracy. He spent all the money he got from his father, who made sweets, and was excited about the future Republic. On September 4th, due to what seemed like a joke, he thought he was made a prefect, but when he tried to start his job, the office workers didn't accept him, and he had to leave. Besides that, he was a nice guy who didn't harm anyone and was very helpful. He worked very hard to prepare the town for defense; he made holes all over the area, cut down trees, and set traps on the roads. When enemies were close, he felt his work was done and quickly went back to the town. He believed he would be more helpful in Havre, where they would need to build more defenses soon.

The woman, known as 'Boule de Suif' or 'ball of fat', was very plump and known for her large size. She was short and round, covered in smooth, shiny skin, and looked very healthy and appealing. Her face was rosy like an apple or a blooming flower, and she had beautiful dark eyes and a small, attractive mouth with white teeth. People said she had many great qualities. When people realized who she was, they whispered words like 'prostitute' and 'public scandal' loudly enough that she noticed and looked at them boldly, making them quiet down, except for a man named Loiseau who kept looking at her with interest.
