Soon, the three ladies there started talking again, feeling united against her because they saw her as a disgraceful figure, while they were respectable wives.

The three men, feeling a natural connection upon seeing Cornudet, began discussing finances with a hint of disdain for those without money. Count Hubert talked about the harm the Prussians had caused him, including the loss of livestock and damaged crops, yet he spoke confidently as a wealthy landowner who could easily recover in a year. Monsieur Carré-Lamadon, experienced in the cotton business, had wisely moved six hundred thousand francs to England to prepare for tough times. Loiseau had managed to sell all his wine to the French government and was now waiting for a large payment from them. The three men shared quick, friendly looks, feeling a bond as wealthy individuals who could hear the sound of money when they reached into their pockets. The journey was so slow that by ten in the morning, they had traveled less than four leagues. They had to get out and walk up hills three times. They were worried because they were supposed to have lunch at Totes and now doubted they would get there before evening. They all started looking for any place to eat along the way when their carriage got stuck in a snowdrift, and it took two hours to free it. As time passed, they became very hungry, which made them feel weak and upset. The presence of the Prussians and the movement of hungry French soldiers had scared away all the local businesses.

The men looked carefully for food in the farms next to the road, but they couldn't find even a piece of bread. The local farmers hid their food because they were afraid the soldiers would steal it, since the soldiers were not given any food and took whatever they could find. Around one o'clock, Loiseau said he was very hungry. Everyone else had been feeling hungry for a while too, and they stopped talking because they were so focused on their hunger. Sometimes, one person would yawn and then others would yawn too. Depending on their personality or how they were raised, they would either yawn loudly or try to cover their mouth quietly. Boule de Suif seemed to be looking for something under her dress a few times. She paused, looked at the others, and then sat back as before. Everyone looked tired and pale. Loiseau said he would pay a lot of money for some ham. His wife almost said something because she didn't like wasting money or even jokes about wasting money. The Count admitted he was also feeling unwell and regretted not bringing any food. Everyone felt bad for forgetting to bring food.

Cornudet had a bottle of rum and offered it to everyone. Most people said no, but Loiseau took a sip and liked it, saying it helped with the cold and hunger. Loiseau then joked they should eat the fattest passenger, like in a song he knew, which upset everyone, especially since it seemed to be about Boule De Suif. No one laughed except Cornudet. The two nuns stopped praying and just sat there quietly, looking sad. At three in the afternoon, while they were traveling through a lonely area with no villages, Boule de Suif quickly got a big basket from under her seat. She first took out a small plate and a fancy cup, then a big dish with two chickens in jelly. The basket also had pies, fruit, and pastries, enough food for three days so she wouldn't need to eat at inns. She also had four bottles sticking out from the food. She started eating a chicken wing neatly with a small bread roll. Everyone stared at her as the smell of the food made them hungry and their mouths water. The women in the carriage began to dislike Boule de Suif even more, almost wanting to hurt her or throw her and her things out into the cold.

Loiseau was looking very hungrily at the chicken dish. 'Madame has thought ahead more than we did,' he said. 'Some people are always prepared.' She looked at him. 'Would you like some, Monsieur? It’s hard to go so long without eating.' He nodded. 'Honestly, I won’t say no. I can’t bear it anymore—it's just the luck of war, right, madame?' He looked around and added: 'At times like these, we’re grateful for any help.' He took out a newspaper, placed it on his lap to protect his pants, and with his knife, he took a chicken leg covered in jelly, bit into it, and chewed with great pleasure. Everyone else looked on sadly. Then Boule de Suif kindly asked the two nuns if they would like some food. They immediately said yes and started eating quickly without looking up, after mumbling a thank you. Cornudet also accepted some food, and together with the nuns, they used newspapers as a makeshift table on their laps. They all ate non-stop, biting and chewing fiercely. Loiseau, who was eating in his corner, quietly urged his wife to eat too. She didn’t want to at first, but then she felt so hungry that she couldn’t resist anymore. Then her husband, speaking smoothly, asked if their 'charming fellow-traveler' would allow him to give some food to Madame Loiseau. 'Of course, Monsieur,' she replied with a friendly smile, and passed him the dish.

There was an awkward moment when they opened the first bottle of red wine because there was only one cup to drink from. Everyone cleaned the cup before passing it on. Cornudet, perhaps being polite, kissed the spot on the cup where the previous person had drunk. Then, while others were eating and the smell of food filled the air, the Count and Countess de Breville and Monsieur and Madame Carré-Lamadon felt very uncomfortable, like the mythical Tantalus who could never reach what he wanted. Suddenly, the young wife of the cotton manufacturer sighed deeply. She turned pale, closed her eyes, and fainted. Her husband was very worried and asked everyone for help. People panicked until one of the nuns helped the fainting lady by giving her some wine to drink from Boule de Suif's cup. The young woman woke up, smiled, and said she felt better, but to avoid fainting again, she was given more wine. The nun said it was just hunger. Boule de Suif, embarrassed, looked at the others who were hungry and started to offer something but stopped, worried they might refuse her.

Loiseau quickly stepped up to help. He said, 'In tough times like these, we should all help each other. Ladies, please don't be shy—take what you can and be thankful. Who knows if we'll even find a place to sleep tonight? At this rate, we won't get to Totes until tomorrow afternoon.' The women were still unsure and no one wanted to say 'Yes' first. Then the Count took charge. He confidently approached the young woman who was offering food and said, 'We gratefully accept your offer, madame.' The hardest part was just starting. Once they began, they eagerly ate everything in the basket. It had many things like a fancy liver pate, a pie made of larks, smoked meat, pears, gingerbread, assorted cookies, and a cup of pickled onions and small cucumbers in vinegar. Boule de Suif, like many women, loved these tangy snacks. After they started eating her food, they felt they should talk to her. At first, they spoke formally, but as they saw she was modest, they became more relaxed. Mesdames de Breville and Carré-Lamadon, who were very cultured, knew how to talk in a friendly yet respectful way. The Countess was especially good at being gracious without seeming superior. However, Madame Loiseau was tough, spoke little, and ate a lot.

The talk shifted to the topic of the war. They shared terrible acts done by the Prussians and how brave the French were; everyone who had escaped praised the courage of those who stayed. Soon, they started sharing their own stories. Boule de Suif explained, with great emotion, why she had to leave Rouen. 'At first, I thought I could stay,' she said, 'because I had enough food at home, and I preferred to feed some soldiers rather than leave my house and not know where to go. But when I saw those Prussians, I couldn't handle it. They made me so angry that I spent the whole day crying from shame. Oh, how I wished I were a man! I watched them from my window—those big, fat men with pointy helmets—and my servant had to stop me from throwing furniture at them. Then some came to stay in my home, and I attacked one of them. I almost choked him but they pulled me away by my hair. After that, I had to keep a low profile. Once I got the chance, I left—and here I am.' Everyone praised her for her actions. She earned a lot of respect from her companions, who hadn't been as brave. As Boule de Suif told her story, Cornudet, who had a long beard and believed strongly in his political views, listened and smiled approvingly, like a teacher who hears a good student. He then spoke in a serious, instructive way, using phrases from daily announcements, and ended with strong words against a political figure he disliked.

But Boule de Suif got very angry because she supported Napoleon. She looked at him with red cheeks and said angrily, 'Ah! I'd like to see you try to do what he did! You would have failed! People like you are the reason he failed. If cowards like you ran the country, we'd all have to leave France!' Cornudet, not bothered, just smiled proudly. However, everyone felt that a big argument was about to start, but then the Count stepped in. He calmed Boule de Suif down by saying that everyone's opinion should be respected. Still, the Countess and the manufacturer's wife, who didn't like the Republic and preferred strong governments, found themselves agreeing with Boule de Suif, even though she was a street woman. The basket of food was empty because ten people had eaten from it, and they wished it was bigger. They talked a bit more, but it was less friendly after they finished eating. As night came and it got darker and colder, Boule de Suif started to feel cold even though she was usually warm. Madame de Breville then offered her a charcoal foot-warmer because it was refilled that morning; she took it quickly because her feet were very cold. Mesdames Carré-Lamadon and Loiseau also lent their foot-warmers to the two Sisters.

The driver turned on his lights, making the horses and the snowy road bright. Inside the carriage, it was too dark to see anything, but suddenly, Boule de Suif and Cornudet moved, and Loiseau thought he saw Cornudet move away quickly as if he had been hit quietly. Small lights appeared on the road ahead. They were arriving in Totes after driving for eleven hours, plus breaks, making it thirteen hours total. They stopped at the Hotel de la Commerce. When the door opened, the sound of a sword hitting the stones made everyone inside scared. A German voice shouted something. Even though the carriage had stopped, no one got out, afraid of being attacked. The driver then shone a light inside, showing everyone's surprised faces. Next to the driver, under the bright light, stood a German officer. He was very tall, thin, and blonde, dressed tightly in his uniform, and wore a small black cap and a very long, thin moustache that made his face look stern.

In a strong French accent, he asked everyone to get off the coach: 'Will you get out, gentlemen and ladies?' The two nuns got off first, used to always following orders. Then came a noble couple, followed by a businessman and his wife, and then Mr. Loiseau, who was pushing his wife in front of him. When he stepped down, he said to the officer, 'Good evening, sir,' more to be safe than polite. The officer just stared back without saying anything.

Boule de Suif and Cornudet, although they were sitting closest to the door, came out last, looking serious and proud in front of the enemy. The young woman tried hard to stay calm, and the man with the long beard kept pulling at it nervously. They both tried to act dignified, knowing they represented their country. They were both upset by how easily the others were cooperating. She tried to act even prouder than the other women, and he continued to show his resistance, which he had started by making traps on the roads.

They all went into the big kitchen of the inn. The German officer, who had their passports signed by the general, looked at each person and checked their details. After a while, he suddenly said, 'That's all good,' and left.

They felt relieved. They were hungry, so they ordered dinner. It would take thirty minutes to get ready, so they went to see their rooms while two servants got the meal ready. The rooms were along both sides of a long hallway that ended in a door with frosted glass. When they were about to eat, the innkeeper came out. He used to sell horses and was a big man who had trouble breathing and always made noises when he breathed. His dad had given him the name Follenvie. 'Is there a Miss Elizabeth Rousset here?' he asked. Boule de Suif was surprised and turned around. 'That's me.' 'Miss, the Prussian officer wants to see you right now.' 'Me?' 'Yes, if you are really Miss Elizabeth Rousset.' She paused, thought for a bit, and then firmly said, 'That may be, but I won't go.' Everyone around her was curious about why she was called. The Count approached her. 'You might regret saying no, madam. It could cause trouble not just for you but for everyone here. It's a mistake to resist those who are stronger. There's probably no danger—it might just be a small oversight.' Everyone agreed with him and tried to convince her because they were worried about the consequences of her refusal. 'Fine, I'll do it for you all!' she finally said. The Countess thanked her warmly. She left the room, and everyone decided to wait for her before they started eating. They all wished they had been called instead of the impulsive young woman and thought about what they would say if they were called next.

After ten minutes, she came back very angry and upset, barely able to speak, and said, 'Oh, the terrible man; he's so mean!' Everyone gathered around her to find out what happened, but she refused to tell them. The Count kept asking, but she proudly said, 'No, it's nobody's business! I can't talk about it.' Then, they all sat down around a big pot of soup that smelled like cabbage. Despite the earlier upset, they had a fun dinner. The Loiseaus and the two nuns chose cider because it was cheaper, and it tasted good. The others had wine and Cornudet asked for beer. He had a special way of opening the beer, watching the bubbles, and checking its color by holding it up to the light. While drinking, his big beard, which was the color of his beer, seemed to move happily. He looked like he was born to drink beer. It seemed like he always thought about his two favorite things – beer and revolution – together. Monsieur and Madame Follenvie sat at the end of the table. Monsieur Follenvie, who was very out of breath and could not talk and eat at the same time due to a chest cold, stayed quiet. However, Madame Follenvie talked non-stop. She talked about when the Prussians arrived, what they did and said, and how much she disliked them because they were expensive and she had two sons in the army. She mostly talked to the Countess, enjoying the chance to speak with a high-class lady.

She quietly started talking about some private issues, and her husband kept interrupting her, saying, 'You should be quiet, Madame Follenvie,' but she ignored him and continued. 'Well, madame, as I was mentioning—these men, they just eat potatoes and pork all day long. And their behavior—! You would see them training for hours and days in the fields—moving forward, moving back, turning here and there. If only they worked on farms or fixed roads in their own country! But no, these soldiers are useless to everyone, and the poor people must support and feed them just so they can learn how to fight. I may be just a simple old woman, but when I see these men exhausting themselves by walking back and forth all day, I think to myself, if some people can make useful things, why do others work so hard to cause harm? Killing anyone, whether they are from here or there, is terrible. If you hurt someone who hurt you, it's wrong and you get punished; but if they shoot our sons like animals, it's seen as okay, and they even reward the person who kills the most. I can't understand or agree with that!' 'War is cruel if you attack someone who hasn't harmed you—it's a duty if you're protecting your country,' Cornudet said loudly. The old woman agreed. 'Yes—defending oneself is different; but wouldn't it be better to stop all these kings who start wars for fun?' Cornudet was impressed. 'Well said!' he exclaimed.

Monsieur Carré-Lamadon was deep in thought. Even though he really admired famous soldiers, he was impressed by the smart ideas of a country woman about how the country could benefit if all the people who didn't have jobs could work on big projects that would take a long time to finish. Loiseau got up from his seat and started talking quietly to the innkeeper. The innkeeper laughed a lot, coughed, and spat, and he agreed to buy a lot of wine from Loiseau for the upcoming spring after the enemy soldiers would have left. After dinner, everyone was so tired that they all went to bed. Loiseau, who had noticed something interesting, let his wife go to bed and then he tried to listen and look through the keyhole to see what was happening in the hallway. After a while, he saw Boule de Suif wearing a blue and white robe, holding a candle, and walking down the hallway. Then a door opened quietly, and after some time, Cornudet in his shirt sleeves came out following her. They spoke softly, and Boule de Suif seemed to be blocking her door, not letting Cornudet in. Loiseau couldn't hear their conversation well until they spoke louder, and he heard Cornudet say, 'Look here, you are really very ridiculous—what difference does it make to you?'

She looked upset and said, 'No! There are times when this is not okay; and here, it would be very wrong.' He did not understand and asked, 'Why?' She got angrier and louder, 'Why? Can't you see why? There are Prussians in the house, maybe even in the next room!' He did not respond. She seemed to challenge his pride, and he quietly went to his own room. Loiseau felt excited and amused, did a little dance, put on his nightcap, and got into bed where his wife was already lying. The house became very quiet. Then, suddenly, a loud, deep, and steady rumbling noise started. It was hard to tell where it was coming from. It sounded like a big machine running: Monsieur Follenvie was snoring.

They planned to leave at eight o'clock the next morning, so everyone gathered in the kitchen by then. However, the coach covered in snow was alone in the courtyard with no horses or driver. They couldn't find the driver in the stables or the coachhouse. The men decided to look for him around the area and left. They ended up in the town square, which had a church on one end and houses along the sides where they saw Prussian soldiers. They saw one soldier peeling potatoes, another cleaning a barber's shop, and a third comforting a crying child. The local women, whose men were away at war, were bossing the soldiers around, showing them chores like chopping wood, grinding coffee, and fetching water. One soldier was even doing laundry for an old woman. The Count was surprised and stopped a church official to ask what was happening.

"Oh," said the old church rat, "these are quite good. They are not from here, and I'm not sure where they're from. They all have families back home. I think their wives and children are missing them. It's sad for them and for us. But right now, we are okay because they are not causing trouble and are working as if they were at home. You see, sir, poor people help each other; it's the powerful people who start wars.",Cornudet, upset by the friendly relationship between the winners and losers, left the area, choosing to stay alone in the inn. Loiseau made a joke, saying, "They are re-populating." Monsieur Carré-Lamadon thought a better way to say it was, "They are repairing." However, they couldn't find the coach driver. Eventually, they found him in the village café, friendly with the officer's assistant. "Didn't you have orders to get the coach ready by eight o'clock?" the Count asked him. "Yes, but I received a different order later," he replied. "What?" "Not to ready the horses at all." "Who told you that?" "The Prussian commander," he answered. "Why?" "I'm not sure—you should ask him. I was told not to ready the horses, so I didn't—there you have it." "Did he tell you himself?" "No, sir, the innkeeper gave me the message from him." "When was this?" "Last night, just as I was going to bed.",The three men were very upset when they returned. They looked for Monsieur Follenvie, but a servant told them he couldn't meet them before ten in the morning because of his asthma. He had even made it clear not to wake him up early unless there was a fire. Then they wanted to meet the officer, but they were told it was absolutely impossible, even though he was staying at the inn.

Only Monsieur Follenvie was allowed to talk to him about non-military things. They had to wait. The women went back to their rooms and kept themselves busy. Cornudet settled in a cozy corner of the kitchen by a big fire. He got a small table and a can of beer, and smoked his pipe calmly. His pipe was very special and important, almost as much as he was to his friends. The pipe was well-used, dark, and smelled nice. Cornudet sat still, looking at the fire and his beer. Every time he drank, he ran his fingers through his hair and cleaned his moustache. Loiseau pretended to stretch his legs but went out to sell his wines. The Count and the manufacturer discussed politics, guessing what would happen to France. They hoped for a hero to save the country when things got really bad. Cornudet listened to them and smiled like he knew all the answers. His pipe made the kitchen smell nice.

At ten o'clock, Monsieur Follenvie came in. Everyone started asking him questions right away, but he only had one response which he repeated a few times. 'The officer told me, "Monsieur Follenvie, tell them they cannot use the horses for the travelers tomorrow morning. They cannot leave until I say so. Understand?" That's all.' They wanted to meet the officer; the Count gave his visiting card and Monsieur Carré-Lamadon added his name and titles to it. The Prussian officer sent a message that he would meet the two men after his lunch, around one o'clock. The women came downstairs and despite feeling worried, they managed to eat a little. Boule de Suif seemed very upset and sick. They had just finished their coffee when an orderly came to take the two men. Loiseau went with them, but when they suggested bringing Cornudet to make their visit seem more important, he proudly said he wouldn't talk to the Germans at all and went back to sit by the fire and ordered more beer. So, the three men went upstairs without Cornudet, and entered the best room of the inn where the officer was. He was relaxing in an armchair, his feet up by the fireplace, smoking a long pipe, wearing a colorful robe probably taken from someone's house. He didn't stand up, greet them, or even look at them, showing off like a typical proud soldier. After waiting a bit, he finally asked, 'What do you want?' The Count spoke for the group. 'We wish to leave, Monsieur.' 'No.' 'May I ask why not?' 'Because I choose not to.'

With respect, Sir, I want to remind you that your general gave us permission to go to Dieppe, and I don't think we've done anything to deserve such harsh treatment from you. "I don't choose—that's all—you can go to town." Everyone bowed and left the room. The afternoon was very unpleasant. They couldn't understand why the German acted this way, and they started to think of all sorts of unlikely reasons. They all stayed in the kitchen, talking nonstop and guessing all kinds of unlikely scenarios. They wondered if they were going to be kept as hostages, or taken as prisoners, or if they would have to pay a lot of money to be free. This idea made them very scared. The richest among them were the most frightened, thinking they might have to give a lot of money to this soldier to save their lives. They tried to think of believable lies to hide their wealth and pretend to be very poor. Loiseau took off his watch-chain and hid it in his pocket. As it got darker, they became more scared. They turned on the lamp, and since dinner was still two hours away, Madame Loiseau suggested playing a card game called "trente et un" to distract themselves. Everyone agreed to play, even Cornudet, who stopped smoking as a polite gesture. The Count shuffled the cards, dealt them, and Boule de Suif won the game of "trente et un" right away. Playing the game helped them worry less. However, Cornudet noticed that the two Loiseaus were cheating at the game.

As they began their dinner, Monsieur came in and said with a rough voice: 'The Prussian officer wants to know if Miss Elizabeth Rousset has changed her mind yet?' Boule de Suif stood up, turned very white, then her face turned red and she was so angry she couldn't talk. Finally, she shouted: 'Tell that mean Prussian—I will not do it—ever! Do you hear me? Never!' The fat innkeeper left the room. Everyone quickly gathered around Boule de Suif, asking her to explain what was going on. At first, she didn't want to say, but then she got so upset that she told them everything. Everyone was so angry that they weren't even shocked. Cornudet slammed his beer glass so hard on the table that it broke. Everyone was cursing at the Prussian, furious, and united in not wanting to give in, as if they all had to make the same sacrifice. The Count said with disgust that these people were acting like savages. The women showed her a lot of kind and warm support. The nuns, who only came out at meals, looked down and stayed quiet. After the initial anger calmed down, they sat down to eat supper, but they didn't talk much and seemed lost in thought.

The ladies went to bed early; the men stayed up to play a card game called ecarté. They invited Monsieur Follenvie to play with them because they wanted to ask him questions about how they could leave despite the officer's orders. But Monsieur Follenvie was so focused on his cards that he didn't listen or answer their questions, and kept saying, 'Play, gentlemen, play!' He was so into the game that he didn't even cough, and his chest made different sounds like musical notes. He didn't want to go to bed when his very sleepy wife came to get him, so she went to bed alone. She had to wake up early the next day, while he liked to stay up late. He just reminded her to keep his chicken broth warm and kept playing his cards. When the men realized they couldn't get any information from him, they decided it was time to go to bed. The next morning, they woke up early, hopeful and eager to leave, but scared of spending another day in the terrible inn. Sadly, the horses were still in the stable and the coachman was nowhere to be seen. With nothing else to do, they walked around the stagecoach feeling sad.

Lunch was very sad, and people started to feel cold towards Boule de Suif. After thinking overnight, they were slightly annoyed with her for not secretly meeting the Prussian to make a nice surprise for them in the morning. They thought it would have been easy and nobody would have known. She could have just told the officer she wanted to stop their suffering. It wouldn't have really changed much for her! But no one admitted they thought this. In the afternoon, because they were very bored, the Count suggested a walk around the village. Everyone dressed warmly and they all went out, except Cornudet who stayed by the fire, and the two Sisters who spent their time in the church or with the priest.

The cold was getting worse every day, biting their noses and ears sharply, and walking was so painful that it hurt a lot. But when they saw the open land covered in white, it looked so sad and endless that they all decided to go back, feeling very downhearted. The four women were walking ahead, with the three men following a bit behind. Loiseau, who understood the situation well, suddenly asked loudly, 'How long was this foolish girl going to make them wait in this place?' The Count, always polite, mentioned that one shouldn't force such a difficult choice on any woman—it had to be her decision. Monsieur Carré-Lamadon noted that if the French army moved towards Dieppe as expected, they would likely meet the enemy at Totes, which worried the others. 'Why not just leave on foot?' Loiseau suggested. The Count dismissed the idea, saying, 'How can you think of walking in this snow with our wives? We would also be caught quickly and taken prisoner by the soldiers.' This argument ended the discussion as it was clear they had no other choice. The women were talking about clothes, but they seemed a bit uncomfortable with each other. Suddenly, at the end of the street, they saw an officer. He stood out against the snow, walking in a way that showed he was trying to keep his boots clean, which is typical for soldiers. When he passed the women, he bowed but only gave a cold look to the men. The men didn't tip their hats, although Loiseau almost did.

Boule de Suif turned red up to her eyes, and the three married women were very embarrassed to be seen with her by the soldier who had been rude to her. They then started talking about the soldier, his looks and his face. Madame Carré-Lamadon, who had met many officers and could judge them well, thought this one was quite good-looking and wished he was French because he would have been a charming hussar and very popular. Once back inside, they were unsure what to do. They argued over small things. The quiet dinner ended quickly and they went to bed early, hoping to pass the time by sleeping. The next morning, they all looked tired and were in bad moods. The women barely spoke to Boule de Suif. Soon, the church bell rang for a baptism. Boule de Suif had a child being cared for by peasants near Yvetot. She rarely thought of the child and saw it only once a year, but the thought of another baby being baptized made her suddenly feel a strong love for her own child. She insisted on going to the baptism. As soon as she left, everyone else started to move their chairs closer to discuss things because they felt it was time to make a decision. Loiseau suggested they ask the officer to keep Boule de Suif and let the others go. Monsieur Follenvie tried to talk to the officer, but was quickly sent away. The German officer, understanding people well, kicked him out and planned to keep them all until he got what he wanted.

Madame Loiseau got very upset. She said, 'We can't just sit here and wait forever! Since that woman usually does this kind of work, she shouldn't refuse anyone. In Rouen, she was with everyone, even cab drivers. I know this for sure because the coachman from the Prefecture told me, and he used to buy wine from our shop. Now, when she can help us escape, she acts all picky—the nerve of her! I think the officer is being very fair. He probably hasn't had the chance for a while, and he could have chosen any of the three here, but he didn’t. He picked the one who is known to everyone. He respects married women. Remember, he is in charge. He could have forced us if he wanted to with his soldiers!' The other two women felt scared. Madame Carré-Lamadon looked nervous and a bit pale, as if she was afraid the officer might grab her. The men, who were discussing this in a corner, came over. Loiseau was very angry and wanted to hand over the woman to the enemy. But the Count, who came from a family of diplomats and looked the part, thought it was better to use cleverness rather than force.

"We need to convince her," he said, and they all agreed to help. The women moved closer, spoke softly, and everyone shared their ideas. They spoke politely and skillfully, using clever language to discuss sensitive topics. A foreigner wouldn't have understood their coded words. Despite appearing modest, the women were actually enjoying the scandalous topic, indulging in the conversation as if savoring a delicious meal meant for someone else. The story seemed so amusing to them that they all cheered up. The Count made some bold jokes that made everyone smile; Loiseau then told some stronger jokes that no one minded, and his wife's shocking suggestion influenced everyone: "If that's her job, why should she say no to one more man?" Little Madame Carré-Lamadon even thought that if she were in that situation, she might be less likely to refuse this man than another.

They took a long time to set up the blockade, as if they were surrounding a fortress. Everyone agreed on their roles, the points they would make, and the actions they would take. They planned how to attack, what tricks to use, and how to surprise the enemy to make them open their gates. Cornudet didn't take part at all. They were so focused on their plan that they didn't notice Boule de Suif entering the room. But the Count whispered 'Hush!' and they all looked up. She was there. The conversation stopped suddenly, and they felt awkward and didn't talk to her at first. Then the Countess, skilled in social tricks, asked her if she enjoyed the baptism. Boule de Suif, still moved by the event, described everything—the people's expressions, their poses, and even how the church looked. She mentioned that it felt good to pray sometimes.

Until lunchtime, the ladies were just being nice to her to make her trust and listen to them more. But when they sat down to eat, they started to really push their ideas. They began talking about being devoted to a cause, using stories from old times like Judith and Holofernes, and others. They made up stories about women from history who had influenced powerful men with their charm and bravery. They talked about this in a subtle and controlled way, but were clearly trying to inspire similar actions. They seemed to suggest that a woman's main role was to always be ready to give up her own desires to support warriors. The two Sisters didn't seem to react and were deep in thought. Boule de Suif didn't say anything. They gave her the whole afternoon to think but started calling her 'Miss' instead of 'Madam', which seemed to lower the respect they had for her and made her feel the weight of her situation.

In the evening, as they were serving soup, Monsieur Follenvie came in again with the same message: 'The Prussian officer wants to know if Mademoiselle Elizabeth Rousset has changed her mind.' 'No, Monsieur,' Boule de Suif answered shortly. During dinner, the group started to get along less well. Loiseau tried to make three jokes, but they didn't go over well; everyone tried hard to think of new topics to discuss but couldn't. Then, the Countess, maybe without planning it and just wanting to show respect for religion, asked the older nun about important events in the lives of the saints. Some saints did things that would be considered crimes today, but the Church forgives these actions if they're done for God or to help others. Then, through a sort of unspoken agreement or maybe because of her simple way of thinking, the old nun strongly supported the group's plan. They thought she was shy, but she turned out to be bold and outspoken. She didn't worry about the little details of right and wrong; her beliefs were strong and clear, and her faith was firm. She thought that if God commanded, she could easily do something drastic like Abraham did when he was willing to sacrifice his son. She believed that God wouldn't mind the action if the intention was good. The Countess used the nun's high regard to further discuss the idea that 'the end justifies the means.' She asked the nun, 'So, Sister, you think God approves of any action that leads to Him, as long as the intention is good?'

Who can question it, Madame? Sometimes a bad action can be good because of the reason behind it. She kept talking about God's will, guessing what God would decide, and involving Him in things that really weren't His concern. She talked in a clever and careful way, but every word from the religious lady in the large white cap broke through the anger of the other woman. Then the topic changed a bit, and the lady with the rosary talked about her religious group, her leader, herself, and her weak friend, Sister St. Nicephora. They were called to Havre to take care of many soldiers sick with small-pox. She explained how bad the soldiers' condition was, and mentioned that while they were delayed by this Prussian's whim, many French soldiers might die, whom they could have helped. Helping soldiers was her special skill. She had worked in Crimea, Italy, and Austria; and as she talked about her experiences, she showed she was like those tough nurses who follow armies, help wounded soldiers during battles, and can calm down rowdy soldiers better than anyone—a real battle-hardened nurse, her face marked by scars, reminding one of the horrors of war. No one wanted to speak after her because they didn't want to ruin the strong impression she made. Right after dinner, everyone quickly went to their rooms and didn't come out until late the next morning. The next day's lunch was quiet. They waited to see if the ideas planted the day before would develop and have an effect.

In the afternoon, the Countess suggested they go for a walk. The Count, as planned, walked with Boule de Suif and started talking to her in a friendly but slightly superior way, as older men often do with young women. He called her 'my dear child' and spoke from a position of high social status. He quickly got to the point and asked her if she preferred to risk danger by not agreeing to do a favor, similar to ones she had done before. Boule de Suif didn't answer. Then, he tried to persuade her by talking about her kindness, her logic, and her feelings. He remained polite and charming, praising the sacrifice she would be making and hinting at their gratitude. He playfully added that the man should feel lucky to meet someone as pretty as her, as he wouldn't find many like her back home. Boule de Suif stayed silent and went back to join the others. When they got back, she went straight to her room and didn't come out again, making everyone very anxious about what she would decide to do. It would be very embarrassing if she continued to refuse. When it was time for dinner, she didn't show up. Monsieur Follenvie then came and said that Mademoiselle Rousset was not feeling well and they shouldn't wait for her to start dinner. Everyone was curious, but the Countess quietly checked with the innkeeper, who confirmed everything was fine. For the sake of keeping it discreet, he didn't say anything to the others but gave them a small nod. Everyone felt relieved and happy.

"Wow!" exclaimed Loiseau, "I'll buy champagne if they have it here!" Madame Loiseau felt very worried when the innkeeper came back with four bottles. Everyone started talking and became happy, feeling like good friends. The Count noticed that Madame Carré-Lamadon was very pretty; the businessman gave compliments to the Count's wife. They all talked a lot, happily and energetically. As the meal ended, the women started making daring jokes. People's eyes shone, they spoke freely, and they had drunk quite a bit of wine. The Count, still looking serious even after drinking, made some clever remarks about how sailors feel when they find a way through the ice at the end of winter. Loiseau, feeling excited, stood up, raised his glass of champagne, and shouted, "To our freedom!" Everyone stood up, cheering loudly. Even the two nuns, after being persuaded by the other women, tried a little champagne, which they had never had before, and said it tasted a bit like lemonade but nicer. "It's too bad we don't have a piano," Loiseau said, wishing they could dance. Cornudet, however, didn't speak or join in the fun at all; he seemed lost in deep thoughts, occasionally pulling on his big beard. Finally, around midnight, as everyone was about to leave, Loiseau, who had had too much to drink, came up to Cornudet, poked him, and said, "You look sad tonight, friend—you haven't spoken at all."

Cornudet became very angry, looked around fiercely at everyone and said, 'What you did today was very wrong!' He then stood up, walked to the door, shouted 'Very wrong!' again and left the room. This made everyone feel a bit down for a while. Loiseau was surprised at first, but then he started laughing loudly and said, 'You're just upset because you didn't get what you wanted!' The others didn't understand what he meant, so he told them a funny story about what happened earlier in the hallway, and everyone started laughing a lot. The women were especially amused, and the Count and Monsieur Carré-Lamadon laughed so hard they cried. They couldn't believe the story. 'Are you saying he wanted to—' 'Yes, I saw it myself!' 'And she said no?' 'Yes, because there was a Prussian soldier in the next room.' 'That's unbelievable.' 'It's true, I swear!' The Count was laughing so hard he almost choked; the manufacturer was holding his sides from laughing. 'And you can guess he didn't find it funny at all tonight—oh, no—not one bit!' And they all laughed even harder, unable to stop. Afterward, they all went to bed. But Madame Loiseau told her husband that Madame Carré-Lamadon didn't seem to enjoy the jokes as much as the others. 'You know how some women love a man in uniform, it doesn't matter if he's French or Prussian. But really, that's a silly way to think,' she said. It seems no one really slept much that night. The lights stayed on late under the doors. They say champagne can do that; it keeps you awake and restless.

The next morning, the winter sun was bright and the snow looked shiny. The stagecoach was ready and waiting outside. A group of white pigeons, with their thick feathers, walked seriously around the horses' feet, looking for food. The coach driver, wrapped in a sheepskin coat, sat smoking his pipe. The happy travelers were busy getting food ready for their trip. They were all waiting for Boule de Suif to come. When she showed up, she seemed upset and shy as she walked towards the other travelers, who ignored her by looking away. The Count, acting very proper, took his wife away from Boule de Suif, as if avoiding something bad. Boule de Suif was confused, but then bravely said 'Good morning, Madame' to the manufacturer's wife, who rudely nodded back without speaking and looked at her as if she was wrong. Everyone was suddenly very busy and stayed away from her as if she was carrying a disease. They quickly got into the coach, leaving her to enter last and sit alone in silence, just like before. They pretended not to see or recognize her, except Madame Loiseau, who looked at her with dislike and whispered to her husband that she was glad not to be sitting next to her. Then, the heavy coach started moving again, and they continued their journey. No one talked at first. Boule de Suif didn't look up. She was angry and ashamed because she had given in to their demands and felt dirty from the kisses of the Prussian soldier they had pushed her towards.

The Countess was the first to speak after a long silence. She turned to Madame Carré-Lamadon and asked, 'Do you know Madame d'Etrelles?' 'Oh, yes, she is a good friend of mine.' 'She's so lovely!' 'Yes, very charming! She's very smart, loves art, sings beautifully, and is excellent at drawing.' The manufacturer was talking to the Count. Even though the window was noisy, you could hear bits of their conversation about business things like shares and profits. Loiseau, who had found an old deck of cards at the inn, started playing a card game called bezique with his wife. The two Sisters took their rosaries, made the sign of the cross, and began praying quickly and quietly, sometimes kissing a medal, crossing themselves, and then continuing their prayers. Cornudet just sat there, thinking. After traveling for three hours, Loiseau put away his cards and joked, 'I'm getting hungry.' His wife then took out a package, opened it, and took out some cold veal. She sliced it and they both started eating.

"What if we do the same?" asked the Countess, and started to take out the food that was packed for both couples. In a long dish with a picture of a hare on top to show there was roast hare inside, there were tasty cold meats—dark slices of juicy deer mixed with other meats. There was also a nice piece of gruyere cheese wrapped in newspaper that still had the words "General News." printed on it. The two Sisters took out a sausage that smelled like garlic, and Cornudet, putting his hands into his big coat pockets, pulled out four hard-boiled eggs from one pocket and a large piece of bread from the other. He removed the egg shells and threw them on the straw under his feet, then started to eat the egg, dropping bits of the yellow part into his long beard, where they looked like stars. In the rush and mix-up of the morning, Boule de Suif forgot to plan ahead, and she watched, very angry and embarrassed, as everyone else ate calmly. A wave of anger shook her, and she opened her mouth to yell at them with the angry words that came to her, but she was too choked up with anger to speak.

No one paid attention to her, no one cared about her. She felt overwhelmed by the negative feelings directed at her by people who had used her and then discarded her like something dirty and worthless. She remembered the big basket of delicious food they had eagerly eaten—two chickens covered in shiny jelly, her small pies, her pears, and her four bottles of wine; and suddenly, her anger cooled off like a snapped string, and she felt like crying. She tried hard to stop herself—she sat up straight and tried to hold back her tears like a child, but tears started to form anyway. They shone briefly on her eyelashes, then two large tears rolled down her cheeks. More tears followed quickly, falling onto her chest. She sat very straight, her eyes staring ahead, her face pale and stiff, hoping no one would see her cry. But the Countess noticed and nudged her husband. He just shrugged as if to say, 'What can you do? It's not my fault.' Madame Loiseau quietly laughed to herself, thinking, 'She's crying because she's embarrassed.' The two Sisters went back to praying after they had packed up their leftover sausages. Then Cornudet, feeling full from his eggs, stretched out his legs, leaned back, smiled like he just thought of a funny joke, and started to quietly whistle a patriotic song. This seemed to annoy the others; they looked upset and tense, as if they wanted to howl in discomfort. He knew this but kept whistling. Sometimes he even sang a few words about fighting for freedom.

Today, they drove faster because the snow was harder. On their long trip to Dieppe, it was very boring and the ride was bumpy. Even as it got dark and then very dark, he kept whistling the same angry tune over and over. This made the tired and annoyed people traveling with him listen to the whole song and remember all the words. Boule de Suif kept crying, and sometimes, she couldn't stop herself from sobbing loudly in the dark.

chapters.title: THE MURDERS IN THE RUE MORGUE - Edgar Allan Poe

paragraphs.content: The thinking skills known as 'analytical' are hard to explain. We only see their effects. We understand that these skills bring great joy to those who have them in large amounts. Like a strong person enjoys showing their strength, someone who analyzes well enjoys solving problems. They find joy even in small tasks that use their skills. They like puzzles and tricky questions, and they solve them in ways that seem almost magical to others. Their methods are so good, their answers seem like guesses.

Studying math, especially its highest part often called analysis, may strengthen our ability to make decisions. However, calculating numbers is not the same as analyzing situations. For example, a chess player can make moves without deeply analyzing the game. People often misunderstand how chess affects thinking. I'm not writing a detailed study, but just sharing some thoughts before telling a special story. I believe that playing the simple game of draughts (checkers) challenges our thinking more than the complicated game of chess. In chess, the pieces move in complex ways which can confuse complexity with depth. You need to pay close attention because a small mistake can cause you to lose. There are many possible moves in chess, increasing the chance of errors. Often, the player who focuses better wins, not necessarily the smarter one. In draughts, the moves are simpler and less varied, reducing the chance of mistakes. The players win by being sharper thinkers. To explain better, imagine a draughts game with only four kings left. No mistakes are expected here. Victory depends only on clever moves which require strong thinking. Without usual strategies, the analyst must fully understand the opponent's thinking and quickly find the best way to win, sometimes using very simple tactics.

Whist is a card game known for improving thinking skills. Smart people enjoy it even though they might find chess too simple. Whist requires strong analytical skills, which means it helps in understanding complex situations. Being good at whist shows you can be successful in other challenging activities where you need to think deeply. In whist, remembering the rules and past plays is important, but being a great player is about more than just following rules. It involves noticing small details and making smart guesses based on those details. For example, a skilled player watches the faces of other players and how they handle their cards to guess their strategies. They pay attention to everything, not just the game, to make the best decisions. This helps them predict the outcome of the game and play their cards with confidence.

Being analytical is different from just being clever. While someone who analyzes well must be clever, a clever person might not be good at analyzing. The ability to combine ideas, which shows cleverness, is thought to be a basic skill by some scientists, but this is probably wrong. This skill is often found in people who are otherwise not very smart, which many writers have noticed. The difference between being clever and being able to analyze is big, much like the difference between having simple creative ideas and having deep imagination, but they are similar in some ways. Usually, clever people are full of creative ideas, and truly imaginative people are always good at analyzing. The story I'm going to tell will help explain these ideas more. When I was in Paris in the spring and summer of a certain year, I met a man named Monsieur C. Auguste Dupin. He came from a very good family, but had become poor because of many bad situations. He stopped trying to improve his situation because he felt overwhelmed. He still had a little bit of money left from his family, and he lived on this money very carefully, just buying what he really needed. The only extra things he spent money on were books, which are easy to get in Paris.

We first met in a small library on Rue Montmartre because we were both looking for the same rare book, and this made us become friends. We met several times after that. I was very interested in his family stories which he shared openly, a common thing for French people when talking about themselves. I was also amazed by how much he had read and was inspired by his lively and creative imagination. While I was in Paris, I felt that having him as a friend was very valuable, and I told him so. Eventually, we decided to live together while I was in the city. Since I had more money than he did, I paid for us to live in a big, old house that matched our moody style, even though it was falling apart and located in a quiet, neglected part of Faubourg St. Germain. If people knew how we lived there, they might think we were crazy, but harmless. We didn't see anyone else while we were there. I kept our location a secret from people I knew before, and Dupin hadn't been in touch with anyone in Paris for many years. It was just the two of us.

My friend had a strange fascination with the night and loved it for its own sake. I always went along with his unusual ideas and enjoyed them completely. We pretended night was always with us. Every morning, we closed all the shutters in our old house and lit some scented candles that gave off a weak light. We spent our time dreaming, reading, writing, or talking until it was really dark. Then we would walk together in the streets, talking or exploring the city late into the night, looking for excitement that just watching quietly could give us. During these walks, I noticed that Dupin, my friend, was very good at analyzing things. He enjoyed using this skill and wasn't shy about saying how much he liked it. He often told me that he could read people easily, as if they were open books, and he proved it to me in surprising ways. When he was like this, he seemed distant and his eyes looked empty. His voice, usually deep, would get higher, but he spoke clearly and carefully. Watching him, I would think about the old idea that each person has two sides to their soul, and I imagined Dupin had two sides too—one creative and one analytical.

Please don't think from what I just said that I am talking about a mystery or writing a story. What I mentioned about the Frenchman was just because he was very excited or maybe not thinking clearly. To understand what he said during those times, I'll give you an example. One night, we were walking down a long, dirty street near the Palais Royal. We were both lost in our thoughts and hadn't spoken for about fifteen minutes. Suddenly, Dupin said, 'He is a very small man, that's true, and he would be better in the Théâtre des Variétés.' I agreed without thinking, because I was so deep in thought I didn't notice how his words matched my thoughts. Soon after, I realized what had happened and was very surprised. I told Dupin, 'I don't understand this at all. I'm really surprised and can hardly believe what I'm seeing. How did you know what I was thinking about?' I stopped to make sure he really knew who I was thinking about. Dupin said, 'About Chantilly, why do you stop? You were thinking to yourself that his small size doesn't suit tragic roles.' This was exactly what I was thinking about. Chantilly used to be a shoemaker on Rue St. Denis, who became obsessed with acting and tried to play the role of Xerxes in Crébillon’s tragedy but was ridiculed for it. I asked Dupin urgently, 'Please tell me how you figured out what I was thinking.' I was even more shocked than I wanted to admit.

My friend said, 'It was the fruiterer who made you think that the shoe repairer was not tall enough for Xerxes and others like him.' I was surprised and said, 'The fruiterer! I don't know any fruiterer.' My friend reminded me, 'The man who bumped into you when we entered the street about fifteen minutes ago.' Then I remembered that a fruiterer with a big basket of apples on his head had almost made me fall as we moved from Rue C into the street where we were. But I didn't understand what this had to do with Chantilly. Dupin, my friend, had no deceit in him. He said, 'I will explain. To make everything clear, let's go back over your thoughts from when I first spoke to you until we met the fruiterer. The main points are—Chantilly, Orion, Dr. Nichols, Epicurus, Stereotomy, the street stones, the fruiterer.' Many people enjoy thinking back on how they came to certain thoughts. It's interesting, and the first time you do it, you're surprised by how disconnected the start and end seem. I was amazed when I heard the Frenchman say what he did, and I had to admit he was right. He then continued:

I remember we were talking about horses just before we left Rue C——. That was the last topic we talked about. As we entered this street, a fruit seller with a big basket on his head quickly walked past us and accidentally pushed you onto a pile of stones that were there because the road was being fixed. You stepped on a loose stone, slipped, and hurt your ankle a little. You seemed upset, said a few words, looked at the stones, and then we continued walking without talking. I wasn't really paying close attention to what you were doing; but lately, I've been noticing things more, almost like I have to.

You were looking down at the ground, noticing the holes and bumps in the road, showing that you were still thinking about the stones, until we reached a small alley named Lamartine. This alley had a special kind of pavement made with overlapping blocks. When we got there, you seemed happier, and I saw your lips move. I'm sure you said 'stereotomy,' a fancy word for this kind of pavement. I remember you couldn't think of 'stereotomy' without thinking about tiny particles and Epicurus's ideas. We talked about how Epicurus's ideas were kind of confirmed by recent space theories, so I knew you would look up at the stars, especially at the Orion nebula. You did look up, and I knew I had guessed your thoughts right. But, in the article from yesterday’s 'Musée' that criticized Chantilly harshly, the writer mentioned the Latin phrase we often talked about, which means 'The first letter lost the old sound.'

I had mentioned to you that this was about Orion, which used to be called Urion. I knew you remembered this because of the way it was explained. So, it was obvious that you would think of both Orion and Chantilly together. I could tell you thought of them together by the smile on your face. You were thinking about the unfortunate cobbler. Until then, you were bending over as you walked; but then you stood up straight. I then realized you were thinking about how small Chantilly was. At that moment, I pointed out that since Chantilly was quite short, he would be more suited to perform at the Théâtre des Variétés. Not long after that, we were reading the evening newspaper, the 'Gazette des Tribunaux,' and we noticed some interesting paragraphs.

Shocking Murders.—Early this morning, at about three o'clock, people living in the Quartier St. Roch were woken up by very loud screams coming from the top floor of a house on Rue Morgue. This house was where Madame L’Espanaye and her daughter, Mademoiselle Camille L’Espanaye, lived alone. When they couldn't get into the house the normal way, neighbors broke the gate with a crowbar. About eight or ten neighbors went in with two police officers. By then, the screaming had stopped; but as they went up the stairs, they heard loud, angry voices from above. When they got to the second floor, it was silent again. The group quickly checked each room. When they got to a big room at the top of the house (they had to break the door open because it was locked and the key was inside), they saw something that shocked and surprised everyone.

The apartment was very messy, with broken furniture scattered everywhere. There was only one bed frame, and the mattress was taken off and placed on the floor. There was a razor covered in blood on a chair. On the fireplace, there were some grey human hair strands covered in blood, looking like they were yanked out. On the floor, they found some gold coins, a topaz earring, some silver spoons, and two bags holding a lot of gold money. The drawers of a desk were open and looked like someone had searched through them, but some things were still inside. They found a small iron safe under the bed, open with a key in it, but it only had some old letters and unimportant papers inside. There was no sign of Madame L'Espanaye in the room, but they noticed a lot of soot in the fireplace. They checked the chimney and found the daughter's body stuck in there, upside down. Her body was still warm, and it looked like she had been pushed up there with a lot of force, which hurt her. Her face and neck had marks and bruises like she had been choked. After looking everywhere in the house and finding nothing more, the group went outside to a small yard behind the building. There, they found the old lady's body. Her throat was cut so deeply that her head came off when they tried to lift her. Both her body and head were badly damaged, almost not looking human anymore.

There is still no clue to this terrible mystery, as far as we know. The newspaper the next day shared more details. The tragedy on Rue Morgue street - many people have been asked about this scary and unusual event, but still, no new information has come out to help solve it. Here is the important information we found out: Pauline Dubourg, a woman who washes clothes, said she has known the two victims for three years because she washed their clothes. The old woman and her daughter got along well and loved each other. They always paid her on time. She didn't know how they made their money, but she thought the old woman told fortunes to earn money. People said she had some savings. She never saw other people at their house when she picked up or dropped off the laundry. She was sure they didn't have a maid. There was no furniture in the house except on the fourth floor.

Pierre Moreau, a shop owner who sells tobacco, said that he has been selling tobacco to Madame L'Espanaye for about four years. He was born nearby and has always lived there. Madame L'Espanaye and her daughter, who have now died, lived in that house for over six years. Before them, a jeweler lived there and rented out some rooms. Madame L owned the house. She didn't like how the tenant was using the house, so she decided to live there herself and didn't rent it to anyone else. The old lady was a bit forgetful. The witness, Pierre, saw the daughter only a few times in six years. They kept to themselves and people thought they had money. Some neighbors said Madame L could tell fortunes, but Pierre didn't believe that. He only saw the old lady, her daughter, a delivery person a couple of times, and a doctor a few times going into the house. Other neighbors said similar things. No one really visited the house often. It wasn't known if Madame L and her daughter had any family. The front window shutters were almost always closed. The back window shutters were also closed except for one in a large room at the top of the house. The house was quite nice and not very old.

Isidore Musèt, a police officer, says he was called to a house at three in the morning. He saw about twenty to thirty people trying to get into the gate. They finally opened it with a bayonet, not a crowbar. It was easy to open because it was a double gate and was not locked at the top or bottom. The loud screams stopped when the gate was opened. The screams sounded like they were from someone in a lot of pain. Musèt led the group upstairs and heard two people arguing loudly on the first floor. One voice was deep and the other was very high-pitched and strange. He heard the deep voice say some French words like 'sacred' and 'devil'. He wasn't sure if the high-pitched voice was a man's or a woman's, but thought it might be Spanish. The room and bodies looked as we described before.

Henri Duval, a neighbor and a silver-smith, agrees with Musèt. They closed the door behind them to keep the crowd out. Duval thinks the high-pitched voice was Italian, not French, and wasn't sure if it was a man's or a woman's voice. He didn't know Italian, so couldn't understand the words but felt it was Italian from the way it sounded. Duval knew Madame L. and her daughter and was sure the high voice wasn't theirs.

Odenheimer, a restaurant owner, offered to give his testimony voluntarily. He doesn't speak French, so he used an interpreter. He is from Amsterdam. He was walking by the house when he heard screams. The screams lasted for about ten minutes. They were very loud and scary. He was one of the people who went inside the house. He agreed with what others said except for one thing. He was sure that the high-pitched voice was a man's voice, and the man was French. He couldn't understand the words being said. The voice was loud and uneven, sounding scared and angry. The voice was rough, not exactly high-pitched. He heard the rough voice say 'sacré,' 'diable,' and 'mon Dieu.' Jules Mignaud, a banker at Mignaud et Fils, Rue Deloraine, is the older Mignaud. Madame L'Espanaye had some money. She opened a bank account with them in the spring, eight years ago. She often put small amounts of money into her account. She didn't withdraw any money until three days before she died, when she personally withdrew 4000 francs in gold. A clerk went with her to her home with the money. Adolphe Le Bon, a clerk for Mignaud et Fils, says that around noon on that day, he went with Madame L'Espanaye to her home carrying 4000 francs in two bags. When the door opened, Mademoiselle L. took one bag from him, and the old lady took the other. He then left. He didn't see anyone else in the street. It was a quiet side street.

William Bird, a tailor, says he was part of the group that went into the house. He is from England and has lived in Paris for two years. He was among the first to go upstairs. He heard people arguing. The deep voice was from a French man. He heard some words but can't remember everything now. He clearly heard the words 'sacré' and 'mon Dieu.' At that time, it sounded like several people were fighting—there was a noise of scraping and scuffling. The high-pitched voice was very loud, louder than the deep voice. He is sure it was not an Englishman's voice. It seemed to be a German's voice, maybe even a woman's. He doesn't understand German. Four witnesses said that the door to the room with Mademoiselle L.'s body was locked from the inside when they got there. Everything was very quiet—no sounds at all. When they forced the door open, no one was inside. The windows in both the back and front room were closed and locked from the inside. A door between the two rooms was shut but not locked. The door from the front room to the hallway was locked, with the key inside. A small room on the fourth floor was slightly open. This room was full of old beds and boxes. They removed and searched everything. They searched every part of the house thoroughly. They even checked the chimneys. The house had four floors and attics. A trapdoor on the roof was nailed down tightly—it looked like it hadn't been opened for years. The time from hearing the arguing to breaking open the room door varied among witnesses. Some said it took as little as three minutes, others said up to five minutes. Opening the door was hard.

Alfonzo Garcio, an undertaker, says he lives in the Rue Morgue and comes from Spain. He was part of the group that went into the house but did not go upstairs. He is nervous and was scared about what might happen. He heard people arguing. The deeper voice was from a Frenchman. He couldn't understand what was being said. The higher voice was from an Englishman—he is sure of this. He doesn't know English, but could tell by the way it sounded.

Alberto Montani, a candy maker, says he was one of the first to go upstairs. He heard the voices too. The deeper voice was from a Frenchman and he understood a few words. It seemed like the person was arguing. He couldn't understand the higher voice. It was fast and uneven. He thinks it was from a Russian. He agrees with what others said. He is Italian and has never talked to someone from Russia.

Some witnesses said again that the chimneys in all the rooms on the fourth floor were too small for a person to fit through. By 'sweeps' they meant the round brushes used to clean chimneys. These brushes were moved up and down all the chimneys in the house. There was no hidden path for someone to go down while others went upstairs. The body of Mademoiselle L'Espanaye was stuck so tightly in the chimney that it took four or five people to pull it out.

Paul Dumas, a doctor, said that he went to look at the bodies early in the morning. They were both on the bed in the room where Mademoiselle L. was found. The young lady's body was badly bruised and scratched. It looked like she had been pushed up the chimney, which caused these marks. Her throat was very scratched up. There were deep cuts under her chin and marks that looked like finger prints. Her face was very discolored, and her eyes were bulging out. Part of her tongue was bitten off. There was a big bruise on her stomach, which might have been caused by someone's knee. Dr. Dumas thinks that Mademoiselle L’Espanaye was strangled by someone unknown. Her mother's body was very badly damaged. The bones in her right leg and arm were broken. The left shin bone and all the ribs on the left side were also broken. Her whole body was bruised and discolored. It's not clear how she was hurt. A big wooden club, a large iron bar, or a chair—something big and heavy—could have caused the damage if a very strong person used it. A woman could not have caused these injuries with any weapon. The mother's head was completely cut off and badly damaged. It looked like her throat was cut with something very sharp, like a razor.
Alexandre Etienne, another doctor, came with Dr. Dumas to see the bodies. He agreed with what Dr. Dumas said and his opinions.

No important information was found, even though many people were asked questions. A murder this confusing and strange has never happened in Paris before—if it really was a murder. The police don't know what to do, which is rare for cases like this. There are no clues at all. The evening newspaper said that people in the Quartier St. Roch are still very upset. They checked the place again and asked more questions, but it didn't help. They also said that Adolphe Le Bon was caught and put in jail—even though there's no new evidence against him. Dupin was really paying attention to what was happening with this case, but he didn't say anything. He only asked what I thought after hearing that Le Bon was in jail. I agreed with everyone in Paris that the murders were a complete mystery. I couldn't see any way to find the murderer.

Dupin said we shouldn't judge just by what seems like a simple check. The police in Paris are known for being smart, but that's all. They don't really have a solid plan; they just react to things as they happen. They show off a lot, but often their actions don't really match their goals, like someone dressing up just to enjoy music better. Sometimes they do achieve surprising results, but usually, they just work hard and stay active. If hard work and activity don't work, their plans fail. Take Vidocq, for example, he was good at guessing and worked hard, but because he didn't think things through well, he made mistakes by looking too closely at the details. He might have seen a few small things very clearly, but he missed the bigger picture. So, you can think too deeply about something. The truth isn't always hidden deep down. In fact, when it comes to really important knowledge, it's usually quite shallow. The truth is more in the low places where we look for it, not on the high places where we find it. When we think about stars, the best way to see them isn't to stare directly but to look from the side using the edge of our eye, which can see faint light better. This way, the star looks brighter. If we look too hard or too directly, we can even lose sight of it completely. Thinking too deeply can confuse and weaken our thoughts, and too much focus can make even obvious things disappear.

About these murders, let's look into them ourselves before deciding what we think. Investigating will be fun, I thought it was a strange choice of words, but I didn't comment. Also, Le Bon once helped me, and I haven't forgotten his kindness. We will visit the place ourselves. I know G——, the head of police, so getting permission won't be hard. The permission was quickly given, and we went straight to the Rue Morgue. This street is a sad, narrow road between Rue Richelieu and Rue St. Roch. It was late afternoon when we got there because it's far from where we lived. The house was easy to find because many people were still standing across the street, staring at its shut windows, just curious. It looked like a typical house in Paris, with a doorway and next to it a small guard booth with a window that could open, showing it was for a caretaker. Before entering, we walked up the street, took a turn down an alley, and then turned again to come up behind the house. Dupin checked out the whole area and the house very carefully, but I didn't understand why.

We walked back to the front of the house, rang the bell, showed our IDs, and were let in by the people working there. We went upstairs to the room where Mademoiselle L’Espanaye's body was found, and saw that the room was still messy. I didn't see anything new beyond what was reported in the 'Gazette des Tribunaux.' Dupin looked at everything carefully, including the bodies. We then checked the other rooms and the yard, with a police officer with us. We finished looking around when it got dark and then left. On the way home, my friend quickly stopped at a newspaper office.

I've mentioned that my friend has many quirks, and I try to accommodate them. He didn't want to talk about the murder until noon the next day. Then, he suddenly asked me if I noticed anything unusual at the crime scene.
The way he stressed 'unusual' made me feel uneasy, even though I didn't know why.
No, nothing strange,

He continued, 'I am waiting for someone who might not have committed the crimes himself, but was somehow involved. He might not be guilty of the worst parts. I hope my guess is right because it helps me solve the mystery. I expect him to come into this room any minute. He might not come, but it's likely that he will. If he does, we'll need to keep him here. Here are pistols; we both know how to use them if we need to.' I took the pistols without really understanding or believing what was happening, as Dupin kept talking mostly to himself. He often speaks in a detached way. He was talking to me, but his voice was like he was talking to someone far away. His eyes looked empty and just stared at the wall.

He said that the voices heard during the argument on the stairs were not from the women, as proven by the evidence. This makes it clear that the old lady couldn't have killed her daughter and then herself. I mention this mainly to be clear and methodical; Madame L’Espanaye wouldn't have been strong enough to push her daughter's body up the chimney as it was found. Also, the injuries on her own body make it impossible that she killed herself. Therefore, someone else committed the murder, and their voices were the ones arguing. Now, let’s focus not on all the evidence about these voices, but on what was unusual about them. Did you notice anything strange? I pointed out that while all the witnesses thought the deep voice was that of a Frenchman, they disagreed about the high-pitched voice, which one person called harsh.

“That was the clue,” said Dupin, “but it was not what made the clue strange. You didn’t notice anything special. Yet, there was something special to see. The witnesses, as you noted, all agreed the voice was rough; they all said the same here. But about the high-pitched voice, the strange thing is not that they didn’t agree—it’s that, while people from Italy, England, Spain, Holland, and France tried to describe it, each one said it sounded like it was from another country. Each one was sure it wasn’t a voice from his own country. Each one thought it sounded like it was from a country they didn’t know well. The Frenchman thought it was a Spanish voice, and said ‘he might have recognized some words if he knew Spanish.’ The Dutchman said it was a French voice; but he didn’t understand French, so they used an interpreter for him. The Englishman thought it was German, and said ‘he doesn’t understand German.’ The Spaniard was sure it was English, but only ‘by the way it sounded,’ since he didn’t know English. The Italian thought it was Russian, but ‘has never talked to someone from Russia.’ Another Frenchman disagreed with the first one and was sure it was Italian, but since he didn’t know Italian, he guessed ‘by the way it sounded.’ How very strange this voice must have been, that such different opinions came from it—voices that even people from the five big parts of Europe found unfamiliar! You might think it could have been the voice of someone from Asia or Africa. There aren’t many Asians or Africans in Paris; but, without dismissing that idea, I just want to point out three things. One witness described the voice as ‘harsh rather than high-pitched.’ Two others said it was ‘quick and uneven.’ No witness could make out any clear words or sounds like words.

Dupin said, 'I'm not sure how much you understood from what I've said. But I believe that even this part of what was said in court about the rough and high-pitched voices is enough to make us suspect something. This suspicion should guide us as we try to solve the mystery. When I talk about 'legitimate deductions,' I mean that these are the only right conclusions to make, and the suspicion naturally comes from them alone. I won't tell you what I suspect yet. Just remember that this suspicion was strong enough to shape my investigation in the room.'

Let's imagine we are in this room. What should we look for first? The way the killers left. We both don't believe in ghosts. Madame and Mademoiselle L’Espanaye were killed by people, not spirits. These people escaped in a physical way. So, how did they escape? There's only one way to think about this, and it should help us decide. Let's look at all the possible ways they could have left. It's clear that the killers were in the room where Mademoiselle L’Espanaye was found, or the next room, when people came up the stairs. We only need to check these two rooms for exits. The police checked the floors, ceilings, and walls thoroughly. They didn't find any hidden exits. But I checked myself too, just to be sure, and found no hidden exits. The doors of these rooms were locked from the inside with keys inside. Now, let's think about the chimneys. The chimneys are too narrow for even a large cat to fit through. So, escaping through the chimneys is impossible. This leaves us with the windows. No one could have escaped through the front room windows without being seen by people on the street. So, the killers must have left through the back room windows. Now that we've reached this conclusion, we shouldn't ignore it just because it seems impossible. We need to show that these 'impossibilities' are actually possible.

The room has two windows. One window is clear of furniture and completely visible. The bottom part of the other window is blocked by a big bed pushed against it. The first window was locked from the inside. People tried hard to open it but couldn't. It had a large hole for a screw and a thick nail was stuck in it. The second window had a similar nail and also couldn't be opened. The police believed that no one escaped through these windows. They decided not to remove the nails or open the windows.

I looked more carefully because I believed the impossible might be possible here. I thought this way: if the murderers escaped through a window, they couldn't have locked it from the inside as it was found. So, the windows must lock by themselves. I checked the clear window, removed the nail with some effort, and tried to open it. It wouldn't open, which I expected. There must be a hidden spring.
