Finding this spring proved my guess right, even though how the nails were involved still seemed strange. I found the spring after searching carefully. I pressed it and decided not to open the window after finding it.

I put the nail back and looked at it closely. Someone who went out through this window could have closed it again, and the spring would have worked, but they couldn't have put the nail back. It was clear to me that the killers must have left through the other window. Assuming both windows worked the same, there had to be a difference in the nails or how they were fixed. I climbed on the bed and closely inspected the second window. I reached behind the headboard and easily found and pressed the spring, which was just like the other one. Then I checked the nail. It was as thick as the first one and seemed to be put in the same way, driven almost all the way in.

You might think I was confused, but if you do, you didn't understand my reasoning. I never lost track of the clues. There was no mistake in any part of my investigation. I followed the secret to the end, and the answer was a nail. The nail looked just like another one in a different window, but that didn't matter because the important clue ended at this nail. I thought something was wrong with the nail. When I touched it, the top and a little bit of the nail came off in my hand. The rest was broken off inside the hole. The break was old because it was rusty, and it looked like it was broken by a hammer hit. I put the top of the nail back where it was, and it looked just like a whole nail again. I pressed something to open the window a bit, and the nail top moved up with the window and stayed in place. I closed the window, and it looked like the nail was never broken. The mystery was mostly solved. The killer had escaped through the window near the bed. The window closed by itself or he closed it, and it locked because of a spring. The police thought the nail had kept it closed, so they didn't look further.

The next thing we need to talk about is how someone could have moved down the building. I felt sure about this when we walked around the building together. There is a lightning-rod about five and a half feet away from the window in question. It would be impossible for someone to reach the window from this rod, let alone get inside it. However, I noticed that the shutters on the fourth floor are a special type called ferrades, which are not commonly used today but can still be seen on very old houses in Lyons and Bordeaux. These shutters look like a regular door but the bottom half has a grid that you can grab onto. These shutters are quite wide, about three and a half feet. When we looked at them from behind the house, they were half open, which means they stuck out from the wall. The police probably looked at the back of the house too, but they might not have thought about how wide the shutters were. They probably didn’t check this area very carefully because they thought no one could have escaped this way. But I could see that the shutter next to the window above the bed could swing open all the way to the wall, getting very close to the lightning-rod, about two feet away. It would take a lot of effort and bravery, but someone could have climbed from the rod to the window this way. If the shutter was fully open, a thief could grab the grid, let go of the rod, secure his feet on the wall, and jump towards the shutter to close it. If the window was open, he might even have jumped inside the room.

I want you to remember that I talked about needing a lot of energy and skill to succeed in a very hard task. I plan to show you that it might have been possible to do it:—but more importantly, I want you to understand that it would take incredible, almost supernatural agility to actually do it. You might think, as lawyers often say, that I should downplay how much energy was needed to prove my point. This might work in law, but it's not how logical reasoning works. I'm only interested in the truth. Right now, I want you to think about the very high level of energy I mentioned earlier and compare it with the very strange, loud, and uneven voice that no two people could agree on where it came from, and that had no clear way of speaking. At these words, I almost understood what Dupin meant. I felt like I was about to understand but couldn't quite get it—as sometimes you're close to remembering something but can't. My friend continued talking.

He explained, 'I changed the focus from how they left to how they entered. I wanted to show that both happened the same way at the same place. Now, let's look inside the room again. It's said that the drawers were searched, but still, some clothes were left inside. The conclusion that someone searched them is silly. How do we know that these were not the only clothes in the drawers? Madame L’Espanaye and her daughter didn't socialize much or go out often, so they didn't need many clothes. The clothes left were as good as any they might have had. If a thief was involved, why didn't he take the best clothes or all of them? And why leave behind four thousand francs in gold just to take some clothes? The gold was left there. Almost all of it was found on the floor. So, forget the idea that the thief came for the money, as the police suggested because of the money delivered to the house. Many coincidences like this happen all the time without us noticing. People who don't understand probability often get confused by coincidences. In this case, if the gold had been stolen, it would have supported the idea of theft for money. But since the gold was left, if we still think the crime was for gold, then the criminal must have been very foolish to leave the gold behind.'

Remember the important things I mentioned—the strange voice, the unusual quickness, and the lack of reason for such a terrible murder. Now, let's look at the murder scene. Here, a woman was killed by being strangled with hands and then pushed into a chimney, head first. Normal killers don't kill people this way. And they definitely don't hide bodies in chimneys. The way the body was pushed into the chimney was very strange and hard to understand, even if we think the killer was very evil. Also, think about how strong someone must be to push a body up into a chimney so hard that it took several people a lot of effort to pull it back out!

Let's look at more signs of incredible strength used. On the fireplace, there were many thick, grey human hairs. These hairs had been pulled out from the roots. You know it takes a lot of force to pull out even twenty or thirty hairs at once. You and I both saw these hairs. The roots had bits of skin stuck to them, showing the huge force used to pull out so many hairs at once. The old lady's throat was not just cut, but her head was completely cut off from her body using just a razor. Please notice how violent these actions were. I won't talk about the bruises on Madame L’Espanaye's body. Monsieur Dumas and his partner Monsieur Etienne said they were caused by a blunt object; they are right about that. The blunt object was actually the stone ground in the yard, where she fell from a window above her bed. This idea, though it seems simple, was missed by the police because they were misled by another detail and thought the windows couldn't have been opened at all.

He said he hadn't claimed it was true, but wanted me to look at a drawing he made on paper. It showed marks on Mademoiselle L'Espanaye's throat described in the testimony as dark bruises and deep marks from fingernails. My friend explained that the drawing showed a strong grip with no slipping, and each finger mark was deep. He asked me to try to match my fingers to the marks on the drawing. I couldn't do it. He suggested that maybe we weren't doing the experiment correctly because the paper was flat and a human throat is round. He gave me a round piece of wood and told me to wrap the drawing around it and try again. I tried, but it still didn't work. I said it didn't seem like a human made these marks. Dupin then told me to read a passage about the large orangutan from the East Indian Islands, describing its size, strength, and wild behavior. After reading it, I immediately understood the terrible nature of the murder.

I finished reading and said, 'The description of the fingerprints matches the drawing exactly. Only an Ourang-Outang like the one described could have made these marks. This bunch of brown hair also looks just like the hair from that type of animal. But I really can't understand all the details of this scary situation. Also, two voices were arguing, and one was definitely a Frenchman’s voice.' 'True, and remember the phrase 'mon Dieu!' that was heard? It was probably a way of arguing or complaining. I think these two words are very important for solving the mystery. A Frenchman knew about the murder. He might not have had anything to do with the actual crime. Maybe the Ourang-Outang got away from him, and he followed it, but couldn’t catch it again because he was too upset. It’s still out there. I’m just guessing here because I’m not even sure about my own thoughts, and it’s hard to explain them to someone else. Let’s just call them guesses. If this Frenchman is innocent like I think, the ad I put in ‘Le Monde’ newspaper last night should make him come to us.'

He gave me a paper, and I read this: CAUGHT—In the Bois de Boulogne, early in the morning on the day of the murder, a very big, yellowish-brown orangutan from Borneo was found. The owner, who is known to be a sailor from a Maltese ship, can get the animal back if he proves it is his and pays some costs for catching and taking care of it. Visit No. ——, Rue ——, Faubourg St. Germain—on the third floor.

“How did you know he was a sailor from Malta?” I asked. “I'm not completely sure,” Dupin replied. “But I found this ribbon that looks like it was used to tie back long hair, like sailors often have. It’s greasy and has a special knot only sailors use, especially from Malta. I found it near the lightning rod. It wasn’t from the people who died. If I’m wrong about the ribbon meaning the man is a sailor from a Maltese ship, it’s okay because I didn’t harm anyone by saying it in the ad. If I’m wrong, he’ll just think I made a mistake and won’t worry about it. But if I’m right, it helps a lot. Even though he didn’t do the murder, the Frenchman will be scared to answer the ad and ask for the Ourang-Outang back. He’ll think: ‘I didn’t do anything wrong; I don’t have much money; my Ourang-Outang is very valuable—it could be like a treasure to me. Why should I risk losing it because I’m scared? I have it right here. It was found far away from where the murder happened. No one will think a wild animal did it. The police haven’t found any clues. Even if they find the animal, they can’t prove I knew about the murder. Also, the ad already said I own the beast. I don’t know how much they know about me. If I don’t claim it, people might suspect the animal. I don’t want to draw attention to myself or the animal. I’ll respond to the ad, take back the Ourang-Outang, and keep it hidden until everything is forgotten.’

We suddenly heard someone walking up the stairs. 'Get your guns ready,' Dupin whispered, 'but don't use them or show them unless I say so.' The front door was open, and someone had come in quietly and started walking up the stairs. Then, they paused for a moment. Soon, we heard them walking down the stairs, but then they changed their mind and came back up quickly and knocked on our door. 'Come in,' Dupin called out, sounding happy. A big, strong sailor walked in. He looked tough but not unfriendly, with a sunburned face partly covered by a beard and mustache. He carried a big wooden club but didn't have any other weapons. He greeted us awkwardly in French with a slight accent. 'Have a seat, my friend,' Dupin said. 'I guess you're here about the orangutan. It's a really impressive animal, and I bet it's quite valuable. How old do you think it is?' The sailor seemed relieved to talk and said confidently, 'I'm not sure, but he's probably around four or five years old. Do you have him here?' 'No, we don't have space for him here. He's at a stable nearby. You can pick him up tomorrow morning. You're ready to prove he's yours, right?' 'Absolutely,' the sailor replied. 'I'll be sad to see him go,' Dupin mentioned.

The man said, 'I don't expect you to go through all this trouble for nothing, sir. I'm willing to pay a reward to find the animal, something reasonable.' My friend answered, 'That sounds fair. Let me think about what I want. I know! My reward will be this. You must tell me everything you know about the murders in the Rue Morgue.' Dupin spoke softly and then quietly walked to the door, locked it, and put the key in his pocket. He took out a gun from his coat and put it on the table calmly. The sailor's face turned red as if he couldn't breathe. He jumped up and grabbed his stick, but then he sat down again, shaking a lot, looking very scared. He didn't say anything. I felt very sorry for him.

My friend," Dupin said gently, "you are worrying too much for no reason. We don't want to harm you. I promise you as a gentleman and a Frenchman, we don't mean to hurt you. I know you didn't commit the terrible acts in the Rue Morgue. But you are somewhat involved. I know things about this case that you wouldn't imagine. Here's the situation: You haven't done anything wrong that you could have avoided. You are not to blame. You didn't even steal when you could have easily done so. You have nothing to hide. You don't need to hide anything. However, you must tell everything you know because it's the right thing to do. There's an innocent man in jail for the crime, and you know who really did it." As Dupin spoke, the sailor calmed down a lot, but he wasn't as bold as before.

He said, 'So help me God, I will tell you everything I know about this, but I don't think you will believe me. Even though I'm innocent, I know it sounds unbelievable. I'll tell the truth even if it's risky.' He explained that he recently went on a trip to the Indian Archipelago. He was part of a group that went to Borneo and traveled inland for fun. He and a friend caught an Ourang-Outang. After his friend died, he was the only owner of the animal. He had a hard time with the animal because it was very wild during their trip back home. Eventually, he managed to bring it safely to his house in Paris. He kept it hidden so his neighbors wouldn't get too curious, until it healed from a foot injury it got from a splinter on the ship. He planned to sell the animal later. One night, after coming back from a night out with sailors, he found the Ourang-Outang in his bedroom. It had escaped from a nearby closet where he thought it was locked up safely. The animal had a razor and was trying to shave itself in front of a mirror, copying what it had seen him do. He was scared to see such a dangerous animal with a razor. At first, he didn't know what to do, but he usually calmed the animal with a whip. When he showed the whip, the Ourang-Outang ran out of the room, down the stairs, and jumped out of an open window into the street.

The Frenchman was very sad as he followed. The ape, still holding the razor, sometimes stopped to make gestures at the man chasing it. It ran away again when the man almost caught up. They chased each other for a long time. It was very quiet in the streets because it was almost 3 in the morning. While running through a small street behind Rue Morgue, the ape saw a light from an open window. It was Madame L'Espanaye's window on the fourth floor. The ape quickly ran to the building, saw the metal rod used for lightning, and climbed up very fast. It grabbed the window shutter, which was open, and swung into the room right above the bed. This whole action took less than a minute. The ape then kicked the shutter open as it went into the room.

The sailor was happy but also worried. He thought he could catch the animal because it was trapped, but he was also scared about what it might do inside the house. He decided to keep chasing it. Climbing a lightning rod is easy for a sailor, but when he reached the window, he couldn't go any further. He could only look inside the room. When he saw what was inside, he was so scared that he almost fell. At that moment, loud screams filled the night, waking up people in Rue Morgue. Madame L’Espanaye and her daughter, wearing their night clothes, seemed to have been working on some papers in a metal chest that was in the middle of the room. The chest was open, and its contents were on the floor. They were sitting with their backs to the window, and it looks like they didn't see the animal right away. The sound of the shutter closing was probably thought to be caused by the wind.

As the sailor looked inside, he saw a huge animal grabbing Madame L’Espanaye by her hair, (which was down because she was brushing it,) and waving a razor near her face, copying how a barber moves. The daughter was lying still on the ground; she had fainted. The old lady's screams and struggles (during which her hair was pulled out) made the peaceful animal become angry. With a strong move of its arm, it almost cut off her head. Seeing blood made it even angrier. It showed its teeth, its eyes looked fiery, and it attacked the girl, grabbing her throat with its sharp claws until she died. At that moment, it saw its owner's face looking scared over the head of the bed. The animal, remembering the whip it feared, suddenly felt scared. Knowing it had done something bad, it tried to hide its actions and started moving nervously around the room, knocking over and breaking furniture, and pulling the bed away from its frame. Finally, it grabbed the daughter's body and pushed it up the chimney, where it was found; then it threw the old lady's body out of the window. As the ape moved towards the window with the old lady's body, the sailor was so shocked that he quickly climbed down the rod and ran home, scared of what had happened and no longer caring about what would happen to the ape. The noises that the people on the stairs heard were the Frenchman's screams of horror mixed with the wild sounds of the animal.

I don't have much more to say. The big ape must have escaped from the room using the rod, just before the door was broken down. It probably closed the window when it went through it. Later, its owner caught it and sold it for a lot of money at the Jardin des Plantes. Le Don was quickly set free after we told the police what happened, with some extra details from Dupin. The police chief, even though he likes my friend, couldn't hide his annoyance about how things turned out and made some sarcastic remarks about people staying out of other's business. 'Let him talk,' Dupin said, not bothering to answer. 'Let him speak; it will make him feel better. I'm happy that I outsmarted him in his own game. But it's not surprising that he couldn't solve the mystery; he tries too hard to be clever which actually makes him miss the point. He's all talk but no substance, like the pictures of the Goddess Laverna, or just all talk and no action, like a codfish. But he's still a good guy. I particularly admire one clever trick he used to gain his reputation for being smart. I'm talking about how he denies what is true and explains what isn't.'
